VI

legendary

FOES
Original PDF page 157

Original PDF page 158

Impossible Foes

T

hreadbearers, mythical monsters, ascended
mortals; ambitious archdevils, ancient
elementals, solemn archangels. Beings
who have ruled a plane of existence for a
thousand years. Creatures born from the
fractured remains of dying stars, grown so
vast in power that entire civilizations pray
to them. Undying warriors that perfected their craft over
centuries, longer than their enemies have been alive.

These are Legendary Foes–the greatest threats a
party of Legendary Characters will ever face, and the
encounters that a campaign of this magnitude is
ultimately built around. Where standard monsters,
however powerful, are obstacles to be overcome, a
Legendary Foe is a force with history, intention, and the
potential to end the journey of even the best prepared
heroes.

Legendary Foes in Your Game

A Legendary Foe is not simply a powerful monster at
the end of a dungeon. These beings are both NPCs and
combat benchmarks–figures with history, motivation,
and a place in the cosmos that exists independently of
the party’s involvement. They were doing something
before the characters arrived, and the world around
them reflects it.

You can incorporate far more than one Legendary
Foe into your campaign; they make for powerful allies,
conditional antagonists, or interested third parties with
their own agendas. In a universe of Legendary
proportions, many creatures populate these tiers of play,
and the party’s relationship with each of them is what
will define the story of your campaign–which they will
quest on behalf of, which they will negotiate with, and
which they will face as the climactic end-of-game boss
battle! D&D is, at its core, a narrative experience that
relies on dice as a way to legitimize the story. Rare is
the table where mechanics take precedence over story,
banter, and theater. The monsters in this book have a
lot of mechanics, but, perhaps paradoxically, this is not
meant to outshine their stage presence. The more
powerful an ability, the more it should be imbued in
flavor and lore. Cinematic descriptions can do miracles
in the way of transforming a complex mechanical
ability, of cryptic and arbitrary appearance, into a
shonen-like moment for your game’s greatest villains. :\

Framework of a Legendary Foe

All Legendary Foes share the general framework of a 5e
stat block, including statistics, actions, features, and
traits. A few factors set them apart:

Numbers. The Legendary Foes present in this book
have thousands of hit points, powerful abilities, and a
plethora of immunities. Only Legendary Characters,
with features that bypass certain 5e concepts, can hope
to face them. Legendary Foes also have different DCs

across their stat blocks; this has already been explained
in the introduction, but it bears repeating–different
abilities warrant varying difficulty levels. Consistent
math is an important feature of low-level play, created to
simplify what a DM has to do and their calculations.
This is not low-level play, and keeping to the expected
DC for a certain creature creates more problems than it
solves.

The maximum ability score for Legendary Foes is 40,
which reflects just how supernaturally mighty some of
these beings are–beyond anything else, including
Legendary Players.

Legendary Point System. Unlike standard Legendary
Resistances and Legendary Actions, Legendary Foes in
this expansion use the LP system detailed in the
opening chapter. This governs their defenses, their most
powerful abilities, and the resource decisions they make
every round. Be transparent with your players about
how many LP the foe has at any given moment–the
entire tactical landscape of the fight depends on it. In
terms of narrative, an experienced hero can tell when
the dragon they’re fighting is out of breath or otherwise
spent; you can use such descriptions to communicate
remaining LPs without breaking immersion.

Every Legendary Foe in this book has been designed
to face a party of Legendary Characters at the
recommended tier of play. This includes the party’s LP,
the full scope of their legendary toolkit, but also the
magic items expected of such a party’s inventory.

Tiers of Play. The creatures in this book are balanced
for a party of 4-5 players in the respective tier of play:
Legend (20th - 21st), Paragon (22nd - 23rd), and
Ascended (24th - 25th). Each tier is a considerable
distance from the next, a gap of about 4 or 5 CR
categories in regular play. This means a Paragon-tier
foe should easily beat a Legend-tier one, special
circumstances excepted. This creates a clear balance of
power between the creatures of your cosmos, directing
you when it comes to what can be an appropriate
challenge for  your party.

Original PDF page 159

Adapting Legendary Foes

Legendary Foes have extensive lore within the Universe
of Strings. When introducing them into your own
campaign, think about what concepts you want to lift
from the narrative to best fit the story your world is
telling.

The mechanical framework we provide is a good
starting point. Yes, with enough work, you could make a
Tarrasque’s stat block fit a human antagonist, but that
requires dismantling the creature and rebuilding it from
the ground up. Instead, start with the creature’s role.
What archetype is this stat block supposed to fill? Is it a
spellcaster or a warrior? A mindless force of destruction
or a calculating warlord? Often, you’ll find our
Legendary Foes can fill several of these archetypes at
once. Choose whichever better suits your game and
build from there.

Only those in bold type are available for this Alpha version.

Vosculd the Avenger, for example, is an undead,
Frankenstein-coded monstrosity–but the stat block can
anchor any story that touches on undeath. A
necromancer’s final form, a legendary death knight, an
Avatar of Death. It can fit the role of a Lich, of the Grim
Reaper, and of the Jailor of the Underworld. Scáthach,
Wielder of Doom, can also fit some of the same
archetypes–read through both stat blocks and
determine which one you like best.

The stat blocks are only constrained by the general
design of the creature itself. The Wyrm of Blasphemy
has two heads, for example–you can use it to represent
any blasphemous dragon if you want, but you will need
to reframe its twin breath weapon and multiattack to fit
your version. Perhaps you’ll find that the Lord of
Anguish is a bit too ethereal to fit your snake-like
bringer of Ragnarok–be sure to change the creature’s
type and more undead-like features!

Original PDF page 160

Grand Alchemist Paracelsion

“You have plunged into the deepest reaches of the

Penumbral Hell. Against all odds, you crossed the

labyrinthine roots of shadow and correctly divined the

location of my laboratory. All of this, you did to find me,

the Grand Alchemist. Was I a fool for believing you

sought me out for knowledge? For the ten thousand

lessons I could teach you about the nature of life,

energy, and matter? Blasted skies, I would be less

disappointed if you asked me to grant you eternal life or

a mountain of gold. Instead, you present me with these…

naive threats of justice.”

The two braziers Paracelsion carries on

his shoulders alight, piercing through

the sorrowful shadows of Sheol

like beams of pure radiance. Every

muscle, every fiber, and every cell

in his body tightens into a battle

stance, as if he were commanding

each of them individually.

“There are seven thousand, nine

hundred and thirty-three alternate

Universes, beyond the veil of the

void. In none of them do you

stand a chance against me.”

– Paracelsion, Master

of Castle Blackfume
Original PDF page 161

Original PDF page 162

The First Art

Legendary is, by and large, a measure of combat ability.
A mage capable of razing a city with a single spell, a
warrior that can cleave through an army and emerge
unscathed, a dragon whose breath weapon can destroy
divine artifacts… that sort of thing. Paracelsion was
never truly considered one of them. He is a scientist of
the highest repute to some, and a monster of the
greatest infamy to others. His battle prowess is almost
an accidental side-effect of his experimentation.

Paracelsion has also been called by other names,
among which his most terrible; Theophrastus, the First
Magi. In that moniker, “First” doesn’t mean he was
before everyone else, but rather that he is ahead of
everyone else.

Of Velenor

Alchemy is the root of all sciences, according to
scholars. That ancient discipline founded by Velenor,
God of Alchemy, sought dominion over reality and
employed transmutation as its foremost tool,
recognizing that all types of matter and energy are, in
fact, one. The two ultimate objectives of most
alchemists were the creation of infinite wealth and the
attainment of eternal life.

The alchemists of the Third Age, in contrast, have
been reduced to meager potion sellers, peddling
draughts of questionable purity to half-baked
adventurers and travelers for a handful of coins. This
follows reason, of course. After Velenor betrayed the
Pantheon of Gods, all of his authored volumes were
burned or heavily censored. Yet, crumbs of this forsaken
art’s true potential remained, scattered across
translations of copies of plagiarisms of the original
texts.

And of course, Velenor was never found after his
betrayal. If one could somehow become his student…
He Who Rekindled Alchemy

Theophrastus, who would later become Paracelsion,
was born the son of a herbalist father and an alchemist
mother. From them, he learned the basics of the craft–
the few principles that survived the censure of Velenor’s
work. And yet, the young human was ambitious, entirely
unsatisfied by the prospect of running a decent elixir
shop and, maybe one day, having multiple locations in a
few mid-sized towns and cities.

Theophrastus felt the call of ancient knowledge and
took up the life of a travelling alchemist and wizard,
conducting deep research in each library, shrine, and
repository he visited, painstakingly piecing back an
impossibly large webwork of erased knowledge. When
he exhausted the information available at each site he
visited, he moved on without a word of goodbye.

A Slow Ascent

His work took decades, but that was hardly a problem
for a man armed with patience and an increasingly wide
arsenal of life-extending secrets. You could fill a
thousand books with the lesser and greater discoveries
penned by Theophrastus during his early travels, and
each of those would be worth its weight in gold.
Throughout his alchemical recipes and signature spells,
there is a glimpse of the primordial truth of alchemy: all
is one, and one is all. What is the difference between
gold and lead? A handful of elements, arranged in some
other manner. What is the difference between the
essence of a God and the soul of a mortal? Merely size
and flow of energy. All can become Divine. It is only a
matter of transmutation…

Original PDF page 163

Brimstone Rebirth

After making one final alchemical breakthrough in his
fabled subnautic laboratory, the alchemist, now a
wizard of great renown and growing infamy, left behind
the material plane and ventured to the infernal plane of
Sheol, where he gathered the materials necessary for a
process he named Alchemical Apotheosis. Many of
those materials were rarer than rare, dating back to the
Age of the Gods. Obtaining them took Theophrastus
plenty of work and service to infernal lords of dubious
morality.

Theophrastus became Grand Alchemist Paracelsion
on the hundredth day of the year 901 A.T. His new form
was that of a Greater Penumbral Fiend, or, in other
words, a demonic demigod, immortal and with
tremendous arcane potential. Rumors tell that this
Alchemical Apotheosis was aided by Velenor himself,
the only being in the Universe capable of such a
breakthrough–before Theophrastus, at least.

Four Centuries of Evil

Paracelsion proved to be as immoral during his
demonic centuries as Theophrastus was during his
human decades, but endowed with knowledge and
power beyond anything he possessed before. While
Theophrastus manipulated the souls of dragons and
devils to create the accursed breed known as Fiendish
Draccus, Paracelsion negotiated with cruel and ancient
wyrms, giving them the secrets of true draconic lichdom
in exchange for knowledge or relics. Each year
Paracelsion roams free, another disastrous alchemical
abomination is loosened upon the Universe.

Those who fancy themselves heroes have taken note
and made the decoupling of his head from his shoulders
a priority. It has, however, proven a frustrating
endeavor, for Paracelsion pays his associates
handsomely and has made friends with a great deal of
villains and morally grey mercenaries, all of whom
command hefty influence across the planes.

To Kill a Shadow

Penumbral Fiends are notoriously difficult to kill, which
surely was a factor Theophrastus considered when
planning his transformation. In addition to all other
problems the task entails, would-be slayers of
Paracelsion need to ensure they have a way of trapping
or destroying his soul, which will rejuvenate in less than
a week if left to its own devices.

It is also safe to assume Paracelsion, who has spent
ten lifetimes in a quest to achieve invincibility, has a few
secret countermeasures prepared against the many
adventurers that come trudging through his shadowy
laboratory-lair in Sheol.

Original PDF page 164

Castle Blackfume

Paracelsion presides over a wandering fortresslaboratory in the penumbral hell of Sheol, where he
harbors dozens of apprentices, whom he instructs in the
ways of alchemy and profane magic. One could hardly
find a more capable teacher for those subjects, although
his disregard for sentient life has led to the death of
many would-be graduates.

Castle Blackfume, as the demonic denizens of Sheol
have taken to calling Paracelsion’s lair, is a difficult
place to find. In fact, that is Paracelsion’s first test for
prospective students and associates. If you are not
capable enough to do so, the Penumbral Alchemist will
likely find you a waste of time anyway.

Finding Castle Blackfume

Paracelsion’s laboratory can either be found through
extreme resilience or blind luck. After all, Castle
Blackfume is no mystical demiplane accessible only by
gathering ancient blessings or artifacts, or any other
spiritual nonsense the Gods were so fond of. It is a
physical location, and quite a noisy one at that, with its
stomping march across the blackened roots of Sheol. If
you are close enough, it’s impossible to miss–you could
simply stumble upon Blackfume while traveling Sheol.

The problem? Well, it can take decades until you
finally cross paths with the walking megastructure, and
navigating or living in Sheol is a challenge in and of
itself. Baleful shadows and penumbral demons stalk its
every corner, and the very roots and blackened karsts of
the plane seem to want nothing more than to strangle
and drown you. For those who want a less uncertain
path to Paracelsion, there is a meld of divination,
abjuration, and good old investigative work that can
light the way for this darkest of quests.
Amid the Dark

The profound shadows of Sheol are obscurity in its
purest, most primordial form. Such penumbra wears
down all that would attempt to pierce it, even with
magic.

Penumbral demons are a violent and sorrowful kind,
and most of them would rather kill than make a deal.
Fortunately, Sheol counts some other denizens,
including fiendish lordlings that raise stout keeps atop
the black stone hills of this inhospitable hellscape. The
few devils that make their home in Sheol are always
down for a deal–almost as down as they are for a
healthy bit of backstabbing. Some of these might even
be willing to reveal Castle Blackfume’s location–for a
hefty fee–and then, almost certainly, sell the heroes out
to the Grand Alchemist himself… A good strategy to
maintain the element of surprise is to kill such an
informant before they have a chance to turn cloak. It’s a
ruthless game, down in the lower planes.

Discovering the Castle’s location is not even half the
task; Sheol is a ruthless plane to traverse, and
attempting to pierce the darkness long enough to find a
path to the Grand Alchemist is a deed worthy of song.
Infiltration

Once the walking fortress is in sight, there remains only
the hardest part; infiltration. The legs of Paracelsion’s
fortress are about six stories high and coated in the
radioactive residue of the structure’s arcane engines.
Flight is a necessity, but not sufficient, on account of the
dozens of hanging turret pods that swing from the
fortress.

Inside, the true scale of Paracelsion’s creation is
revealed: mazes of clanking gears, amid engines and
reactors, attended to by slaves and deadly constructs.
Only in the deepest part of the fortress, where
laboratories and personnel quarters are found, does
Paracelsion conduct
 his prestigious classes. The
Grand Alchemist’s
 apprentices are talented
like few wizards in
 the entire cosmos.
Original PDF page 165

Original PDF page 166

Grand Alchemist

Paracelsion
Huge fiend (penumbral), neutral evil

Armor Class 22 (natural armor)

Hit Points 502 (31d12 + 300)

Speed 50 ft.

STR

22 (+6)
18 (+4)
30 (+10)
25 (+7)
23 (+6)
20 (+5)

Saving Throws Con +20, Int +17, Str +16
Skills Arcana +27, Perception +16
Damage Resistances acid, cold, fire, lightning, thunder
Damage Immunities poison; bludgeoning, piercing, and
slashing from nonmagical attacks that aren’t silvered
Condition Immunities charmed, frightened, exhaustion,
poisoned
Senses darkvision 480 ft., truesight 30 ft., passive Perception
26
Languages All
Recommended Level 21st - 22nd

Proficiency Bonus +10

Limited Magic Immunity. Paracelsion cannot be affected or
detected by spells cast at 3rd level or lower unless he wishes
to be. He has advantage on saving throws against all other
spells and magical effects.

Penumbral Sight. Paracelsion’s darkvision is not impeded by
magical darkness.

Regeneration. Paracelsion regains 100 hit points at the start
of his turn while his heart remains within his chest.
Paracelsion only dies if he starts his turn with 0 hit points
and doesn’t regenerate. Paracelsion is immune to any effects
that would prevent him from regaining hit points or reduce
his maximum hit points while his heart is in his chest.

Philosopher’s Stone. Paracelsion’s blood is the very elixir of
life, and his heart a tireless engine that keeps it flowing.
While his heart remains within his chest, Paracelsion doesn’t
age nor require food, air, drink, or sleep to subsist.

While Paracelsion has less than 200 hit points remaining, his
heart, a chunk of brilliant golden rock, becomes exposed.

The heart is a medium object lodged in Paracelsion’s chest
with 100 hit points, 25 AC, resistance to piercing and
slashing damage, and immunity to fire, poison, and psychic
damage. If the heart is destroyed, it breaks apart, revealing a
small, palm-sized Philosopher’s Stone that falls to the
ground. A creature can only target the heart if it is exposed. If
Paracelsion has 0 hit points and the heart is destroyed,
Paracelsion’s body melts into black ooze.

A creature within 5 feet of Paracelsion can attempt a DC 30
Strength (Athletics) check to remove the heart, if it is visible.
The DC is lowered to 20 if Paracelsion has 0 hit points
remaining. On a success, the creature rips the heart from his
chest, and Paracelsion takes 35 (10d6) necrotic damage.
While carrying his heart, Paracelsion can put it back in its
place as a bonus action, and it instantly regains its
functionality.

Ritual Savant. Paracelsion can cast all ritual spells from the
Wizard spell list, but only as rituals.

Telekinesis Master. When Paracelsion casts the Telekinesis
spell, the spell’s range is increased to 120 feet, and its
maximum manipulable weight for objects is increased to
2000 pounds.

Legendary Caster. Paracelsion can concentrate on two spells
simultaneously. If he fails a concentration check, he loses
concentration on all spells he is currently concentrating on.

Legendary Foe

Paracelsion has 3 Legendary Points (LP). He can use these
points to fuel certain abilities. Once he uses a feature or
action that consumes a Legendary Point, he cannot use that
same ability again until the start of his next turn. Paracelsion
regains spent Legendary Points at the start of his turn.

Legendary Resistance. When Paracelsion fails a saving throw,
he can expend 1 Legendary Point to succeed instead.

Legendary Endurance. When Paracelsion takes damage, he
can expend 1 Legendary Point to reduce the damage taken
by 100.

Cleanse Effect. If Paracelsion starts his turn affected by a
condition or magical effect of 7th level or lower, he can
expend 1 Legendary Point to end the effect. If the effect or
condition is of 8th or 9th level, or from a Mythical Technique,
Paracelsion must expend 2 Legendary Points to end that
effect. Effects originating from a 10th-level spell cannot be
cleansed in this way.

Original PDF page 167

Alchemical Spellcasting

Grand Alchemist Paracelsion is a 23rd-level spellcaster. His
spellcasting ability is Intelligence (spell save DC 25, +17 to
hit with spell attacks). Paracelsion has the following spells
prepared:

At will. darkness, lightning bolt, slow, telekinesis

3/day each. dimension door, disintegrate, dominate person

1/day each. incendiary cloud, maze, teleport

Actions

Multicast. Paracelsion uses his Spellcasting. He then makes
two Blaze attacks. Instead of using his spellcasting,
Paracelsion can choose to make two Claw attacks. At any
point, Paracelsion can choose to drop his Alchemical
Braziers and replace each Blaze attack with a Claw attack.

Blaze. Ranged Spell Attack: +17 to hit, range 120 ft., one
target. Hit 21 (6d6) force damage. Paracelsion can only use
this attack while holding his Alchemical Braziers.

Claw. Melee Weapon Attack: +16 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.
Hit 23 (3d10 + 6) slashing damage. Instead of dealing
damage, Paracelsion can choose to grapple the target
(escape DC 24). Paracelsion can grapple up to 2 creatures in
this way. If he chooses to drop his Alchemical Braziers, he
can grapple up to 4 creatures in this way.

Bonus Actions

Penumbral Shift. Paracelsion teleports, along with any
equipment he is wearing or carrying, to an unoccupied space
he can see within 60 feet.

Transmuting Touch (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion channels his
transmuting might to all creatures grappled by him. If a
target is a nonmagical object, it is transmuted into another
object or material of Paracelsion’s choice that has roughly
the same mass. If a target is a creature, it must succeed on a
DC 25 Constitution saving throw or take 65 (10d12) force
damage. If this damage brings a creature to 0 hit points,
Paracelsion can choose to destroy the body and transform it
into a material of its choice, such as a pile of sand, a stone
statue, or a pool of slime.

Reactions

Alchemical Shield. As a reaction to an attack that would hit
him, Paracelsion creates an alchemical shield around
himself, increasing his AC by 5 until the start of his next turn.

A creature that hits Paracelsion with a melee attack while
this shield remains takes 14 (4d6) acid damage. Paracelsion
can only use this reaction while holding his Alchemical
Braziers.

Intercept. As a reaction to a creature Paracelsion can see
entering his melee reach, he can make a Claw attack against
it.

Superior Counterspell (Costs 1 LP). As a reaction to a creature
it can see within 60 feet casting a spell, Paracelsion casts
Counterspell at 8th level, potentially nullifying the spell.

Extra Actions

Paracelsion can take extra actions outside of its turn by
expending Legendary Points, choosing from the options below.
Only one extra action can be used at a time and only at the end
of another creature’s turn.

Blaze (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion makes two Blaze attacks.

Claw (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion makes one Claw attack.

Telekinetic Action (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion casts Telekinesis on
himself. If Paracelsion is already under the Telekinesis spell,
he exerts his will on a creature or object within range.

Transmute Air (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion casts Fly on himself
and moves up to half his fly speed.

Transmute Fire (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion casts Flame Strike.

Transmute Earth (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion casts Wall of Stone.

Transmute Water (Costs 1 LP). Paracelsion casts Cone of Cold.

End Magic (Costs 1 or 2 LP). Paracelsion casts Dispel Magic at
5th level if he expends 1 Legendary Point, or at 9th level if he
expends 2 Legendary Points.

Flames of the First Art (Costs 3 LP). Paracelsion spins his
Alchemical Braziers in a wide circle, igniting a large area
beyond his immediate reach. Creatures within 10 feet of
Paracelsion must succeed on a DC 20 Dexterity saving throw
or take 30 (4d10 + 6) bludgeoning damage. Creatures further
than 10 feet from Paracelsion but within 60 feet of him must
make a DC 25 Dexterity saving throw, taking 35 (10d6) acid
damage, 35 (10d6) cold damage, 35 (10d6) fire damage, 35
(10d6) lightning damage, and 35 (10d6) thunder damage on
a failure, or half as much damage on a success. Paracelsion
can only use this action while holding his Alchemical
Braziers.

Original PDF page 168

The Inner Laboratory
Lair Actions

Paracelsion’s personal research facility and living
quarters are located at the innermost part of the
fortress, in a gargantuan room whose centerpiece is a
massive arcanite reactor–some say the largest in the
Universe, which he can only power thanks to a deal he
cut with a horrid, aberrant lord by the name of Arkan,
who provides the penumbral alchemist with several
metric tons of arcanite every week, in exchange for
assorted favors. The two villains help each other further
their own agendas.

Theseus’ Notes on Legendary Foes
Paracelsion, like so many other villains of this Universe, is
no true ally of our cause, even if he studied under Velenor.
He knows you, dear Eternity, and he knows the cold truth of
what we strive for. Like Arkan, the worst traitor among your
ranks, he has built an empire of influence and wealth, and
will fight tooth and nail to protect it from your… sweet
oblivion. Yes, his methods and long list of crimes may be
unpalatable to the heroic types that have made foiling us
their reason to live, but oh… Trust me, they will put their
differences aside when the going gets rough, these little
animals. Survival instinct is a powerful thing.

Among the villains that will turn cloak when you start
dealing eternal nothingness instead of power, Paracelsion is
one of the most dangerous. I know you have felt his gaze,
dear Eternity, watching across your blessed field of void,
seeking what is beyond. Seeking other realities. This power
and knowledge are what he will share with his newfound
allies when he feels threatened by our advance.

Fortunately, we can have some heroes slay him for us! No
champion of good is fond of any of Theophrastus’ many
duplicates, much less of the Penumbral Alchemist, who has
single-handedly committed more atrocities than anyone in
myth–including Ariadne and myself!

When fighting inside his inner laboratory, Paracelsion
can control its many mechanisms and arcane
contingencies to take lair actions. On initiative count 20
(losing initiative ties), Paracelsion takes a lair action to
cause one of the following effects. Paracelsion cannot
use the same lair action twice in a row. The spell save
DC for any spells cast as a lair action is 20, and contests
regarding them are resolved using Paracelsion’s
Intelligence modifier.

Displace. Hidden circles of teleportation appear
beneath two creatures of Paracelsion’s choice within his
lair, causing them to swap places. He can target himself
with this lair action.

Cage. Paracelsion casts the forcecage spell anywhere
within his lair, which lasts until he uses another lair
action.

Unravel Arcana. Paracelsion creates a 30-foot radius
sphere of anti-magic centered anywhere within his lair,
which lasts until he uses another lair action. The field is
an antimagic field spell.

Transfusion. Bloody circles of transmutation appear
beneath all creatures of Paracelsion’s choice within his
lair. Targets must succeed on a DC 20 Constitution
saving throw or take 21 (6d6) necrotic damage.
Paracelsion regains hit points equal to the total damage
dealt.

Prana Drain. Sulphuric circles of transmutation
appear beneath a creature of Paracelsion’s choice
within his lair. It must succeed on a DC 20 Constitution
saving throw or lose a spell slot of 7th level, or the
highest level spell slot it has available of 6th level or
lower, if it has no 7th level spell slots.

Rain of Acid. Paracelsion creates an acid shower in a
60-foot-high, 30-foot radius cylinder centered anywhere
within his lair. Creatures within that area must make a
DC 20 Dexterity saving throw, taking 35 (10d6) acid
damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful
one.

The Grand Alchemist’s Final Act. Paracelsion
triggers a catastrophic arcane meltdown in the reactor
at the heart of Castle Blackfume, dooming the structure.
Once Paracelsion uses this lair action, he cannot use
lair actions again. At initiative count 20 on each
subsequent round, all creatures and objects within the
fortress take 65 (10d12) radiant damage; a creature can
make a DC 20 Constitution saving throw to take half
damage instead. After 10 minutes, the reactor fails
entirely. Every creature and object within 10,000 feet of
Castle Blackfume takes 175 (10d6 x 5) fire damage,
175 (10d6 x 5) radiant damage, and 175 (10d6 x 5)
force damage.

Original PDF page 169

DM Notes

Paracelsion encapsulates the very pinnacle of an
alchemist’s power, provided with mighty spells and
transmutative effects that make reality his playground.
To an unprepared party, the sheer versatility and
incapacitating potential of such a foe will quickly prove
a deadly threat.
A Mighty Lair

The considerations in this section assume your players
will be facing Paracelsion within his lair, where there is
much to do for the party other than assassinating the
fiend. This fight becomes a whole lot easier if that is not
the case! Dealing 500 points of damage and succeeding
in interrupting Paracelsion’s regeneration should be
within the capabilities of any legendary party, or even
well-optimized parties of level 17-20. In such cases,
consider giving your players a lot less information about
Paracelsion’s abilities before the fight. He will need the
element of surprise as an edge, without his beloved lair
and helpers.

Preparation & Information

If you intend to have your party beat Paracelsion as part
of your game, they simply must know some things in
advance. Chiefly, they need to know how to surprise
him. If Paracelsion is allowed to prepare, the party will
find him surrounded by his apprentices, along with two
dozen other countermeasures, afforded to him by his
vast wealth of resources.

Getting the drop on Paracelsion, along with all the
preparation and investigation such a task requires, must
be part of the legendary adventure that culminates in
this fight. In a certain way, the battle begins weeks or
even months before the showdown at his lair.
Outwitting a paranoid, deathless alchemist is no easy
task!

We suggest that further information, such as what his
mightiest spells and actions, or perhaps his capability of
destroying his lair in an apocalyptic meltdown as a last
resort, be handed out as rewards for exceptionally good
investigating done by the party.

Brushes With Death

With only 502 hit points, Paracelsion has a pronounced
weakness against parties with very high damage output
that can outpace his regeneration, overcome his
resistances, and negate his contingencies. The fight is
designed to have him lowered below half his maximum
hit points with relative ease, to showcase his
exceptional regenerative ability. And indeed, that is the
flavor this legendary foe should evoke! In addition to his
regular regeneration, the Transfusion lair action will aid
him, should too many players remain in his field of view
simultaneously.

Paracelsion’s heart is the source of his immense
regenerative abilities. The real battle begins when the
party understands this and starts attempting to remove
or destroy it.

Paracelsion, Legendary Mage
If you read Theseus’ Tome of Legendary Foes, you may
remember Paracelsion quite differently, with a massive spell
list, including a 10th-level spell, and a considerable capacity
for spamming lower-level spells. This is no longer the case,
and there are a couple of reasons for that: first of all, we
wanted to simplify the decision-making process for DMs
running Paracelsion, which was excessively marred in
possibilities, in the previous composition. Secondly, a
power budget that relies mainly on spells is almost
impossible to balance–the mere presence of a character
that has counterspell prepared is enough to take much
difficulty off the encounter. And at this level, your party may
either have superb protections against spells, or very few,
depending on their classes, items, and chosen heroic feats,
causing them to be wiped out quite anticlimatically. Thus, a
more alchemy-focused Paracelsion was developed for this
book, still full of magic, but with fewer pure spellcasting
options.

However, if your party is particularly spell-resistant, of
higher level, or you simply want a more arcana-focused
Paracelsion, consider giving him more spells. Here’s an
alternative Alchemical Spellcasting list we recommend for
that purpose:
Greater Alchemical Spellcasting
Grand Alchemist Paracelsion is a 23rd-level spellcaster. His
spellcasting ability is Intelligence (spell save DC 25, +17 to
hit with spell attacks). Paracelsion has the following spells
prepared:

At will. banishment, counterspell, darkness, lightning bolt,
slow, telekinesis

3/day each. dimension door, disintegrate, dominate person,
hold monster

1/day each. incendiary cloud, feeblemind, maze, teleport

1/day, costs 2 LP. theophrastus’ flux

Original PDF page 170

Vosculd, The Avenger

“Oh, Dante…

You curse me as a blight born of the malignancy of

necromancers. You swear oaths of vengeance when I

lay waste to the villages under your protection. You

promise that your hammer will one day bash my skull to

pieces. And best of all, you fail to realize that when you

insult me, it is very the fruit of your labor that you are

disparaging.

After all… I could not be here without you. Your

efficiency is what drove those fetid necromancers to

retrieve pieces of Death from scorched Aurora. Your

pride is what drove you into their trap, where they

almost killed you… and took a sample of your giant-

borne lightning. Your zeal is what blinded you to their

ultimate goal: creating me–your doom.

But don’t worry, paladin. I’ve ended them. A perk of

my divine progeny: there will be only darkness in their

afterlife. I know you do not see it this way, Dante–but I

am your successor. I will take my horde to Iskendar and

avenge our ancestors. You know you’ve always wanted

to do that… you’re simply too held down by honor. What

honor was there in your exile? You will join me in this

crusade, I know. Willingly or not… you shall be at my

side when I decapitate the Holy Reverend and consign

his soul to the lightless pit where it belongs. You will

bear witness to the greatness of your successor.”

– Vosculd, Avenger of Giants and Avatar of Death
Original PDF page 171

Original PDF page 172

Death Looms Anew

The Third Age seems increasingly unstable. Heroes and
villains of great ambition appear in every corner of the
Universe, each seeking to bring about era-culminating
events. Among these is the blight known as Vosculd, an
undead entity of great and terrible power that has
recently emerged in the forsaken lands of Irxe Vynn.

This haunted region was once a powerful vampire
nation, destroyed by heroes of the light thousands of
years ago. The horrors the vampires brought upon those
wretched hills have remained, however, and the ruins of
Irxe Vynn have never failed to attract those interested in
the blasphemous art of necromancy.

In recent times, the once disorganized undead
hordes, thus far kept at bay by independent holy orders,
have become astoundingly ordered, pliable to the will of
this so-called “Vosculd”, and strange new undead are
being created through its divine necromantic power.

A Call For Help

This anomaly of a new Undead Lord was faced by none
other than Dante Val’Athur, the de facto commander of
the undead-slaying orders of the region. Even he, the
famous Paladin of Storms, had to retreat from
Vosculd’s onslaught.

An arcane missive was then sent to the many powers of the
material plane, but few responded, and none pledged
effective help. Extraplanar powers were called to
intervene. Of these, only the City of Strings heeded the call,
sending scouts led by Zaiah Fleshbinder, Threadmaster of
Necromancy and Restoration, to investigate the origins of
this monster.

What she found in the grey earth of Irxe Vynn was
unexpected, to say the least. The undead scourge of the
region is vampiric in origin: thralls of the fallen vampire
lords, occasionally forming hordes that can threaten a
nearby realm, but not much more than that. This “Vosculd”
character was different, and to Zaiah, it was immediately
clear why: it was Divine. This blighted, sadistic being had
within it the greatest accumulation of the Divine Domain of
Death ever recorded.

The Divine Domain of Death

To understand the significance of Vosculd–called Death
Incarnate–it is important to understand the nature of
Death as a divine domain, as a fundamental force of reality.

Contrary to popular belief, Death is not rooted in
violence, murder, or even the act of dying itself. Rather, it
is an existential state: the mortal soul in transit between its
mortal life and its immortal afterlife. The spirit in its most
pure, incorporeal and vulnerable form. In essence, Death
is the time when a soul is but a dash of white flame,
awaiting for either circumstance or a greater power to
shape it into a new, immortal afterlife. In this state, bad
actors can easily take control of the soul and use it for their
nefarious purposes.

During the First Age of the Universe, Anubis was the
God associated with this state of being; the Keeper of the
Dead, successor to his father Osiris, and protector of souls.
He ensured this transient state led to the appropriate
afterlife, be it angelic, demonic, or some other fate in the
middle.

Death Torn Asunder

The end of the First Age saw Eternity, the Enemy of All,
ravage reality with its void armies. To stop it, the Gods
sacrificed themselves in one last charge, creating a seal
powerful enough to keep Eternity outside of reality. Mighty
and dutiful, Anubis was called to this final battle, and when
he was destroyed by the energies of the void, his Divine
Domain of Death almost went with him. For the concept of
Death to remain within the bounds of Existence and not be
swallowed by Eternity, it had to be violently cut away from
Anubis in an event that, supposedly, brought Mazdyr, God
of Justice, to tears. It’s said all mortals felt a profound
heartbreak in that moment, being unable to explain its
source, as their keeper was slain.

The Divine Domain of Death was torn asunder as a
result of this separation. It splintered into well over a
thousand pieces, ethereal bouts of power that were
scattered across the Universe.

pages.name: p. 173 - Many landed in Aaru, Anubis’ planar realm, where his

text.content:
Original PDF page 173

Many landed in Aaru, Anubis’ planar realm, where his
surviving servants fashioned them into beings known as
the Angels of the Grave, reapers of overdue souls and
enforcers of the rules of Death.

Others landed in the material plane, among a
community of mortals called the Gora, who worshipped
Anubis. They were Teng, the first and mightiest mortal
race, and they too inherited some of Anubis’ power,
becoming Death-wielding Clerics.
The Birth of Necromancy

The rest of the splinters of Death were disseminated far
and wide, and most were picked up by opportunists
seeking dominion over the now guardianless Domain of
Death. In any case, the gate was open for the monstrous
manipulation of mortal souls, not only by wielders of
Anubis’ power, but by mages far and wide, who found
that souls, unguarded, could be held captive through
magic.

This discovery led to an unbearable reality: souls
could be harnessed, enslaved, and raised as undead
servants, regardless of their will. Dying meant being at
the mercy of your killer. It was the birth of Necromancy;
without a guardian, the souls of the dead were preyed
upon and subjugated. Liches and necromancers
multiplied like fungi on a rotting forest–the work of
Anubis came undone.

Thus, among mortal communities, Necromancy
became the greatest crime one could commit, and
paladins of religious fervor started hunting down the
powerful wizards who cultivated such cursed powers.
Great holy wars defined the so-called Dark Years after
the Fall of Anubis, until the largest one of them all,
commanded by Atizai the Green, spelled the end of
Belm, the greatest necromacer lord of those troubled
times. Without him, the undead camp suffered a swift
but bitter defeat.

Death, Risen Anew

Over seven thousand years have passed since the fall of
Anubis. Death is still without a guardian, but
necromancers are much less common, relegated to the
dark recesses of the worlds, as mortal civilizations
declared necromancy a capital offense. And through it
all, the Splinters of Death have subsisted, being used by
diverse peoples for purposes noble and ruinous alike. At
last, this brings us back to the present day, where an
undead lord without equal in living memory seems to
control–and indeed, be– the greatest repository of
Anubis’ lost divinity.

The planar scouts of the City of Strings have worked
on identifying Vosculd’s origins and potential
weaknesses. As far as weaknesses go, there has been
little progress. Origins, on the other hand, have been
determined with absolute certainty.

Zaiah’s Notes On Death
The Gora Teng, distant ancestors of mine, were true friends
of Osiris and Anubis–this explains why, after the God of
Death’s tragic demise, much of the Divine Domain of Death
wound up in their hands. The might the Tomb Clerics of
Gora commanded was so great that the superpower of the
Second Age, the Numarii Empire, taxed them at a much
lower rate than most other imperial provinces, keen to
maintain a good relationship–they knew what the Wielders
of Death could bring, as part of the Numarii army.

The fall of Numar by the hand of its own Emperor saw
formerly colonized peoples take up arms against their
weakened Teng masters, including those of faraway
provinces. The Gora, in the likeness of other Teng provincial
capitals, built a massive arcane engine to defy gravity itself
and took their city of Gorashai to the sky, wherein the cults
of Anubis remained alive and thriving.

For thousands of years, the Gora Teng kept to themselves,
as the marvelous Gorashai floated across the world,
unbothered by the politics of the lands below. As the adage
goes, however, all that goes up must eventually come down,
and it came true for Gorashai in the year 201 A.T. Their
great engine was sabotaged by a yet-unknown criminal, and
thousands of years of culture and progress were lost in a
fiery wreck.

The crash site of Gilded Gorashai was once a lush,
swamp-like region in the continent of Amanis. When the
floating capital collided with the ground, however, the
splinters of Death carried by the Gora cults sank into the
earth, turning water to sand, killing all that lived for miles
and miles around. The resulting wreck was named the
Aurora Desert–a region of black sand where spirits linger
and bodies refuse to decompose. It also happens to be the
homeland of the Albiri, successors of the Gora–my people.

From the remains of Death came a variety of powerful
beings named the Vdekja… among which I count myself.
None of us wield more than a twentieth part of the original
Domain of Death, according to Threadmaster Fallow’s
estimations. This Vosculd monster is the sole exception. He
wields a full fifth of it…

– Zaiah Fleshbinder, Threadmaster of Necromancy &
Restoration

Original PDF page 174

Of Dante Val’Athur

It all starts with a particular half-giant paladin; Dante
Val’Athur, an exile from the Kingdom of Iskendar and
an exceptionally deft, battle-hardened commander. Sent
to the wastes of Irxe Vynn as a disgraced knight, Dante
found kinship among the undead-slaying orders of the
region, surging through the ranks and coming to lead
the 77th Chapter of the Hopelands.

Following a doctrine of complete extermination, his
efficiency and zeal were a threat to a powerful caste of
necromancers that used the lawlessness and chaos of
Irxe Vynn as cover for their nefarious dealings. United
in purpose, they decided to destroy Dante Val’Athur in a
way that would make it impossible for his loyal clerics
to resurrect him. This required the power of the Divine
Domain of Death. The Cabal, as they dubbed
themselves, sent powerful agents across the world.
Their mission was to retrieve as many splinters of
Death as possible, primarily from the ruins of the Gora
civilization, which once harbored the greatest mortal
cults to Anubis in the material plane.

Seven years after departing Irxe Vynn, they returned,
weathered but successful, to deliver Death itself to the
masters of the Cabal.
The Cabal, Decimated

By the time the agents returned, carrying numerous
shards of Death within ornate arcane cages, the
coalition of paladins led by Dante had decimated the
Cabal’s leadership. Of the dozen masters of the dark
arts that founded the organization, one remained:
Kharizza the Apothecary, considered the weakest
member of the twelve.

Kharizza knew she could not hope to wield Death
herself. Maybe if she had a decade to prepare… but she
had mere months until Dante’s paladins found her
necromantic laboratory. Thus, she decided on another
route: what Kharizza lacked in raw prana, she made up
for in resources of all kinds–this was her role in the
Cabal, a dark apothecary who could supply almost any
component, to fuel even the rarest rituals.

Building Vosculd

Using resources from her vast collection, as well as
some fetched by the Cabal agents that brought Death to
her, she labored for a hundred days, and on the
hundred-and-first, was ready to unveil the flesh-golem
that would wield Death in her name, and use it to snuff
out Dante’s lightning forevermore. In Kharizza’s
research notes, her ideas are written with harrowing
plainness. Her failure loosened a terrible villain upon
the world, but her success might have been even
worse…

Death needs a formidable casing, a physiological

container worthy of its power: the carcass will be that of

a giant. Death needs a wielder powerful enough to tap

into its wondrous capabilities: the carcass will be

animated with giant’s lightning, taken from Dante

himself by the Cabal. Finally, Death needs to be mine: a

gem in its heart and a ring on my finger, bound by the

Rite of Angel’s Blood, to secure obeisance. The Storm

Paladins will be the first to feel my servitor’s blade, but

all the chapters will follow, until Irxe Vynn is restored to

its former glory. I will be the new Nightlady, like the

Vampires of old, and the entire continent of Amanis will

pledge allegiance to me, in life… or in death.

– Kharizza, Apothecary of the Cabal

Death Awakens

Kharizza spared no expense in crafting her perfect
avenger, who would wield Death in her name: bones of
an old giant hero, muscles of the toughest undead flesh,
and weapons of the green arcane metal known as
Belmite. All cleaved together and animated by the living
lightning of the near-extinct Storm Giants, stolen from
Dante Val’Athur himself in an ambush, back when the
paladin chapters and the Cabal were more evenly
matched.

With the control ring in her index finger, she had her
agents drive two large steel spikes into the
abomination’s chest and pulled a lever to inject the
primal lightning into it. The hand twitched first, then the
left leg. Finally, its mouth contorted into a twisted grin,
and ignoring Kharizza’s telepathic orders, Vosculd
lunged forward at the speed of thunder and baptised its
green blades with the blood of its would-be master.

From “Examinations”, Section 77-X
What we found in Kharizza’s laboratory was absolute
carnage. Reading some history would have served her well:
she was far from the first mage to have the brilliant notion
of turning a Wielder of Divinity into a puppet. In principle,
it’s not the worst idea: you get to use the might of a Divine
Domain while preventing its power from consuming you.
Divine domains, however, have a volition of their own.

It is likely that Vosculd immediately saturated the mental
pathway between master and thrall with his raw divine
power. Opening your mind, even to a slave, creates a
vulnerability. Stupefied, Kharizza likely couldn’t even feel the
blades of Vosculd ripping her apart.

–Guilliam Fallow, Threadmaster of Arcana & Science

Original PDF page 175

Old Memories

As it turned out, animating the corpse of a hero of
giantkind with a giant’s lightning was more effective
than Kharizza could’ve imagined. The moving carcass
could remember many things of its past life: its status as
a noble warrior, its exploits, even its demise at the
hands of the humans under King Roland. They say that
without a soul, the memories of the mind are
inaccessible. The power of Death overcomes that
metaphysical limitation, as demonstrated by this
creature.

It is impossible to know whether the old giant hero
Vosculd is in control of Death, or if Death is simply
puppeteering its body and usurping its memories. What
is certain is that the creature that resulted from this
unholy union wields divine power, and is motivated by
old grudges. The giants called their fall at the hands of
mankind “the Bane”, and Vosculd has repeatedly
proclaimed itself an avenger of that genocide. Strangely,
it has also vowed to eradicate necromancers and
paladins, the entire nation of Iskendar, and a dozen
groups besides. A confusing melange of vengances
swirls in Vosculd’s mind, a true puzzle for the scholars
of this wounded Universe.

Mounting Threat

Alas, studying Vosculd’s strange fusion of volitions is a
task for another day, for it has used its divine power to
bind the meandering souls of long-dead giants to rotting
corpses, creating mighty revenants under its service.
This process is slow–most modern mortal bodies
cannot accommodate the soul of a giant, and thus
Vosculd is forced to desecrate old pantheons for
mightier vessels. The precious time afforded by this
quest must be used to a single end: destroying Vosculd
and containing its divinity, before it grows strong
enough to raze neighboring Iskendar and put millions of
innocents to the sword.

Hark, self-styled heroes. You are here to bring about my

end, I understand. But if you truly are heroes, then let us

converse for a moment. Perhaps bloodshed can be

avoided. My heart aches under the weight of the

infamies committed against my forebears. In my divine

soul, I remember my own death; the death of a God.

Deep within my skin are the scars of Roland

Giantsbane’s torment. This carcass I inhabit was flayed

to death, you know? The lightning that gives my muscles

motion also remembers. It remembers how the

honorable inquisition of Iskendar stripped me of land

and titles for the crime of having a few drops of giant’s

blood in my veins.

Do you remember? Do you weep for the lost dream of

Anubis? Then join me, would-be heroes. Help me bring

Death to the unworthy, and lead the world into a Just

Age.

No?

Well, then. Your corpses will make fine vessels for my

avengers.

– Vosculd, the Avenger

Theseus’ Notes on Legendary Foes
This Vosculd character, dear Eternity, is a marvelous
testament to the work you’ve done in the Universe. To think
that this is the greatest heir to Anubis’ power! He who foiled
a dozen ploys to corrupt mortalkind! Oh, how the mighty
have fallen. Despite its laughable weakness and adorable
confusion, the rise of Vosculd does put us in a quandary,
I’m afraid. You see, my dear Eternity, if we let it continue on
its path of destruction, it may very well wound Eos and
ripen it for our taking… or it may unite the powers of the
First Circle against a frankly weak foe, and create the
fraternity needed to resist our advance later. Worse yet, I’ve
heard tell of a stringer heroine, a certain Zaiah Fleshbinder
of Aurora, who apparently already wields a sizeable fraction
of the Domain of Death. If Ariadne were to procure
Vosculd’s own fraction and bestow it upon her, well… we
may yet face a true Goddess of Death, risen anew.

Recruiting Vosculd to our cause and directing its rage at
our enemies is a possibility, but my recommendation is that
we send an agent to slay it forthwith. Its splinters of Death
can be erased, or better yet, given to rapturous, chaotic
wielders. This divine unity will only beget resistance.
Effective resistance, for Vosculd is not clever enough to be
sneaky.

Original PDF page 176

Vosculd, the Avenger

Large undead, neutral evil

Armor Class 25 (natural armor)

Hit Points 920 (80d10 + 480)

Speed 60 ft.

STR
DEX
CON
INT
WIS
CHA

22 (+6)
28 (+9)
23 (+6)
16 (+3)
24 (+7)
15 (+2)

Saving Throws Str +16, Dex +19, Con +16, Wis +17
Skills Acrobatics +19, Perception +17, Intimidation +12
Damage Resistances bludgeoning, piercing and slashing
from nonmagical attacks
Damage Immunities lightning, necrotic, poison
Condition Immunities exhaustion, poisoned
Senses blindsense 120 ft., passive Perception 27
Languages celestial, giant, camadarian, teng
Recommended Level 20th - 21st

Proficiency Bonus +10

Limited Magic Immunity. Vosculd can’t be affected or
detected by spells of 3rd level or lower unless it wishes to be.
It has advantage on saving throws against all other spells
and magical effects.

Soul of Death. Vosculd has a piece of a divine domain in
place of a soul. It is immune to spells, magical effects, and
conditions that would manipulate its soul or reduce its
maximum hit points.

Stormcast. Vosculd can tap into the lightning that animates it
to cast spells. Wisdom is its spellcasting ability for these
spells (spell save DC 25, +17 to hit with spell attacks).

At will: lightning bolt

3/day each: chain lightning

1/day each: storm of vengeance

Giant’s Lightning. Damage dealt by Vosculd ignores
resistance to lightning damage.

Evasion. When Vosculd is subjected to an effect that allows it
to make a Dexterity saving throw to take only half damage, it
instead takes no damage if it succeeds on the saving throw,
and only half damage if it fails.

Blades of Belmite. Vosculd’s Arm Blade attacks are magical
and deal an additional 26 (4d12) necrotic damage (included
in the attack).

Master of Souls. When a mortal creature dies within 1000 feet
of Vosculd, it can choose to capture the creature’s soul using
a reaction. An unwilling soul can attempt to escape its grasp
by succeeding on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw. Vosculd
can choose to share its senses with a soul captured in this
way, allowing it to see and hear as if they shared a body. It
can also allow it to speak with its voice.

While Vosculd lives, souls captured by it can only be freed by
its will or through a Wish spell cast at 10th level. Vosculd can
choose to keep captured souls in complete unconsciousness
or create an illusory environment for them to inhabit, which
can take any shape Vosculd chooses but cannot exceed an
area equal to a 15-foot-tall, 15-foot-wide cube. In this
miniaturized afterlife, souls can be kept in luxury and
relaxation, or in terrible torment. All captured souls are freed
in the event of Vosculd’s destruction.

Soul Binder. Completing a 10-minute ritual, Vosculd can bind
a mortal soul it captured to a new body. The body must have
belonged to a humanoid of at least CR 5 or, if it had class
levels, a total level of at least 5. The returned creature
becomes a Revenant (5e SRD) or other type of undead, at the
DM’s discretion. When the ritual ends, Vosculd can choose
to control the creature, creating a thrall, or allow it to
maintain its free will.

Splinters of Death. Vosculd carries within itself thirteen
shards of the Divine Domain of Death. In the event of its
demise, they will blossom from its corpse as pale, senescent
roses, ready for another to claim. A Splinter of Death will
only attune to a creature that embodies its values with
exceptional depth. A creature that attempts to claim a
Splinter forcefully must make a DC 25 Charisma saving
throw, gaining control of the Splinter on a success, or taking
105 (6d6 × 5) force damage on a failure. The DC increases by
5 for each Splinter after the first, up to a maximum DC of 50.
A creature that fails to claim Divinity in any way cannot
attempt to do so again for the next 24 hours.

Psychic Fracture. Vosculd is vulnerable to psychic assault.
When Vosculd takes 20 psychic damage or more, it gains
one charge of Psychic Fracture.

1 Charge: Vosculd cannot use reactions.

2 Charges: Vosculd cannot use bonus actions.

3 Charges: Vosculd’s maximum speed is halved.

4 Charges: Vosculd is Impaired.

5 Charges: Vosculd is Stunned.

Vosculd loses all charges of Psychic Fracture at the end of
each of its turns.

Legendary Foe

Vosculd has 3 Legendary Points (LP). It can use these points
to fuel certain abilities. Once it uses a feature or action that
consumes a Legendary Point, it cannot use that same ability
again until the start of its next turn. Vosculd regains spent
Legendary Points at the start of its turn.

Legendary Resistance. When Vosculd fails a saving throw, it
can expend 1 Legendary Point to succeed instead.

Legendary Endurance. When Vosculd takes damage, it can
expend 1 Legendary Point to reduce the damage taken by
100.

Cleanse Effect. If Vosculd starts its turn affected by a
condition or magical effect of 7th level or lower, it can
expend 1 Legendary Point to end the effect. If the effect or
condition is of 8th or 9th level, or from a Mythical Technique,
Vosculd must expend 2 Legendary Points to end that effect.
Effects originating from a 10th-level spell cannot be cleansed
in this way.

Actions

Multiattack. Vosculd makes three attacks: two with its Arm
Blades and one with its Bite. It can forgo its Bite attack to
cast a spell from its Stormcast list instead.

Arm Blades. Melee Weapon Attack: +19 to hit, reach 10 ft., one
target. Hit: 22 (2d12 + 9) slashing damage plus 26 (4d12)
necrotic damage.

Bite. Melee Weapon Attack: +19 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target.
Hit: 20 (2d10 + 9) piercing damage. If the target is a
creature, it must succeed on a DC 25 Constitution saving
throw or be poisoned for the next minute. A creature
poisoned in this way takes 45 (10d8) necrotic damage at the
start of each of its turns.

Whirling Blades (Costs 2 LP). Vosculd performs a frenzied,
spinning blade dance that lasts until the start of its next turn.
While performing this dance, Vosculd’s AC is increased by 5.
When the dance begins, creatures within 10 feet of it must
make a DC 27 Dexterity saving throw, taking 26 (4d12)
slashing damage and 52 (8d12) necrotic damage on a failed
save, or half as much on a success. For the duration of the
dance, a creature that enters that area for the first time on a
turn must make the same saving throw or take the same
damage. A creature can only be damaged by this effect once
per turn. The dance ends early if Vosculd is stunned,
incapacitated, or impaired.

Bonus Actions

Blitz. Vosculd magically moves up to 240 feet in a straight
line to an unoccupied space it can see, momentarily
becoming a bolt of savage lightning. While moving in this
way, Vosculd can move through objects and creatures.
Creatures within the area it traverses must make a DC 25

Dexterity saving throw, taking 21 (6d6) lightning damage on
a failed save, or half as much on a successful one.

Consume Soul (Costs 1 LP). Vosculd consumes a soul it
captured using its Master of Souls trait. It regains an amount
of hit points equal to five times the total CR or character
level of the destroyed soul, minimum of 10. A creature
whose soul was destroyed in this way cannot be resurrected
by any means short of a Wish spell cast at 10th level.

Reactions

Call of the Afterlife. As a reaction to a creature it can see
rolling a death saving throw, Vosculd imposes a -10 penalty
to the roll.

Capture Soul. Vosculd captures the soul of a creature that has
died within 1000 feet of it as per its Master of Souls trait.

Censor Necromancy. When a spell of the Necromancy school
is cast within 120 feet of Vosculd, it can use a reaction to
nullify it. The spell slot and action used to cast it are still
expended.

Extra Actions

Vosculd can take extra actions outside of its turn by
expending Legendary Points, choosing from the options
below. Only one extra action can be used at a time and only
at the end of another creature’s turn.

Move (Costs 1 LP). Vosculd moves up to its maximum speed.

Bite (Costs 1 LP). Vosculd makes one Bite attack.

Death Stare (Costs 2 LP). Vosculd regards a creature within
120 feet with the sight of death itself. The creature becomes
paralyzed for an hour. A creature can make a DC 25
Constitution saving throw at the start of each of its turns,
ending the effect on itself on a success. Undead creatures
are not affected by this ability.

Invoke Death (Costs 3 LP). Vosculd draws upon the power of

Kill. All creatures within 60 feet of Vosculd are targeted by the
Power Word Kill spell.

Wail. A torrent of wailing mortal souls is unleashed in a 120foot-radius sphere centered on Vosculd. Creatures within
that area must succeed on a DC 25 Charisma saving throw
or take 35 (10d6) necrotic damage and gain 1d4 + 1 levels of

it dies.

Raise. Vosculd casts the True Resurrection spell innately,

Original PDF page 178

DM Notes

Vosculd is a Legendary Foe that has a varied and
powerful offensive repertoire, but is comparatively meek
in terms of defense. This makes timing and strategic
thinking all the more important in a confrontation with
it: the ability to deal devastating but non-incapacitating
damage might result in fast casualties, once Vosculd
gets its retort. Players facing it at the intended tier of
play (20th to 21st level) will find deadly threat at every
step of the confrontation. Preparation and proper DM
warning are very important.

A Hazy Reckoning

Vosculd is a creature born not long ago, created in a
blasphemous rite that endowed it with a melange of
broken memories from Anubis, the massacred Gora
Teng priests, some ritually sacrificed cultists of the
Aurora desert, and a thousand mortals besides. Its soul
thirsts for violent retribution. The force of its avenging
will stands in direct contrast to its focus: with such
varied and hazy enemies to bring a reckoning to,
Vosculd has thus far contented itself with raising an
army of undead to excoriate the guilty when the time
comes.

Depending on which point of its vengeful crusade the
party finds it in, Vosculd’s understanding of the world
and the meaning of its objectives can vary greatly,
creating possibilities for peaceful resolutions or more
bitter battles. Vosculd’s willingness to suppress its killer
instinct and establish dialogue will also depend on party
composition, as it searches for any of those responsible
for the many woes it has endured.
The Returned

With the power of its partial divinity, Vosculd can build
an army. Its Soul Binder trait and Invoke Death extra
action allows it to not only resurrect the oldest creatures
of your world, but also raise an army of undead thralls
under its command. It can also, however, allow raised
creatures their free will if it finds their motivations
aligned with its own vengeful wishes.

Creatures resurrected by Vosculd that are left their
free will may choose not to follow the Avenger, leading
to interesting prelude encounters with deadly revenants.

This also means that the more time passes, the
mightier Vosculd becomes, as the dead are raised to be
part of the Avenger’s army.

Mechanics

Despite its general existential confusion, Vosculd is no
fool. In battle, it will be keenly aware of the windows of
opportunity afforded by its powerful array of abilities.
Specifically, this means that Vosculd will try to use its
many damage-dealing abilities to chip away at the
party’s overall health until several of them are bloodied
enough to be affected by Power Word Kill.

Some considerations will greatly aid an attentive
party. Chief among them is space. Vosculd’s chip
damage is best when dealt as an AoE, and denying it of
that possibility will make it that much less effective.
Stormcast and Blitz, for example, become much weaker
when the party creates distance between its members
and is careful not to provide Vosculd with an easily
exploitable line. Another great boost to their chances
can come via a fourth-level spell: Death Ward. This
spell, easily cast on the entire party before the
confrontation even begins, can save party members
from one instance of Power Word Kill. Because of the
Censor Necromancy reaction, it will be hard to reapply
this effect once it’s gone. Finally, Vosculd has a
weakness that is directly baked into its stat sheet: its
mechanical vulnerability to psychic damage. If the party
can find a way to deal a consistent amount of psychic
damage, the difficulty of the fight can be reduced quite
drastically, much like fighting a vampire using sunlight.
Souls

It is up to you, as the DM, to determine how many
powerful souls Vosculd has gathered before the party
faces it. Consume Soul is the only way Vosculd can
regain hit points during the fight, and the number of
meaningful uses available–each consuming a CR 15-20
soul for 75 to 100 hit points of healing–can significantly
affect the difficulty of the encounter.

This gives you a useful balancing lever mid-battle.
You are not required to disclose how many souls
Vosculd has in reserve; as far as the party is concerned,
it could have an inexhaustible supply. If the fight is
going too smoothly, a few additional CR 20 souls
waiting to be consumed can restore the intended
tension without changing any other aspect of the
encounter. Do remember that it still takes Vosculd a
bonus action to regain hit points in this way, and taking
a couple instances of psychic damage can be enough to
nullify the creature’s healing factor.
Changing Flavor

Should your particular version of Vosculd not be a
Frankenstein-esque monster animated by lightning, we
recommend you change all instances of lightning
damage in this stat sheet to necrotic damage. This is
often enough to fit a role that frames Vosculd as a pure
Lord of Undeath.

Original PDF page 179

Fighting Venue
Tiers of Play

Vosculd has no lair to speak of, having abandoned the
putrid laboratory of its birth immediately upon slaying
its creators. It wanders, along with its followers, seeking
the components to multiply and bring about its
vengeance. The party can face Vosculd in wildly varied
environments, all of which imply a different level of
urgency to its defeat. Below are some suggested arenas
and what they could mean:

Catacombs of Irxe Vynn

Dark, humid, and full of forgotten terrors, the
Catacombs of Irxe Vynn are a series of ancient
structures where Vosculd will find the corpses it needs
to raise its most loyal followers from the afterlife.
Fighting it here means the party is confronting the
menace early. There will be few lackeys to worry about,
Vosculd will be less articulate if spoken to, and it’ll
possess fewer souls to raise or consume. Use the cover
of darkness and Vosculd’s massive blindsight range to
make up for its confusion, and this will feel like a
thrilling horror hunt.
Plains of the Hopelands

Locked in war against the men and women commanded
by Dante, the Paladin of Storms, Vosculd will have a
better idea of what it is, of what it wants, and of who it
must kill to achieve its vengeance. A small but loyal
following of powerful resurrected giants will be at its
side, making it more difficult to deal with Vosculd
quickly and cleanly.
Razed Iskendari Village

Breaking into the kingdom of Iskendar, Vosculd will be
quite sure of its purpose on this godforsaken earth.
Putting villages to the sword one by one, its blight has
become impossible to ignore. There is little chance for a
peaceful resolution at this stage of its story–too much
blood has been spilled already, and Vosculd has raised
enough revenants to become a formidable Undead
Lord. Vosculd must be destroyed.
Ruins of Gorashai

Finally, after Vosculd slashes through southern
Iskendar, the armies of the powerful kingdom will unite,
forcing it to retreat. Headed to the Aurora Desert with a
few loyal followers, Vosculd will search for the
remaining Splinters of Death found in the ruins of
Gilded Gorashai. For a party trying to slay it, this
confrontation becomes a matter of preventing the
ascension of the villain. Should they fail, an existential
threat will emerge from these ruins.

Vosculd is designed in such a way that it will be quite
outmatched by parties beyond its intended tier: it has
plenty of LP spenders, a comparatively modest
maximum hit points, and a power budget that hinges on
its high LP cost abilities. This balance works well at the
intended tier, but becomes difficult to balance at
Paragon-level. Here’s what we recommend:

Legend

There are a few considerations to make in this tier, as it
is the intended one. Check if your party has access to
abilities, spells, or items that disproportionately target
Voscul’s weaknesses–psychic damage-dealing weapons,
access to the Death Ward spell in masse, etc. On their
own, these advantages are expected and even
necessary, but if too many accumulate, they may
trivialize the fight.

Paragon

To scale Vosculd for the second tier of legendary play,
it’s often enough to increase its maximum hit points and
add an additional Legendary Point to its arsenal. This
small change has an extraordinary effect on Vosculd,
who can now both use a Legendary Resistance and the
Invoke Death extra action in the same round. In
preparatory rounds, wherein it’ll be more focused on
doing chip damage and trying to incapacitate as many
party members as possible, this additional Legendary
Point will also help it drain the party’s pool of
Legendary Points.

Vosculd greatly benefits from having powerful
revenants at its side. Sometimes, a strong enough lich
or death knight fighting alongside Vosculd can change
the difficulty of the battle drastically.

Ascended

At the third tier of legendary play, Vosculd starts to be
truly out of its depth. For a properly playtested and
curated experience, we discourage it as a choice for
parties this mighty. If it does come down to it, Vosculd
can pose a threat alongside another Legend-tier enemy,
perhaps a resurrected paragon of old. If Vosculd stands
alone, and you wish for it to be a proper Ascended-tier
challenge, increase the creature’s hit points to 2000 and
add 2 Legendary Points to its arsenal. Depending on
your party’s strengths, some additional changes might
be required.

Original PDF page 180

The Heirs of Leung

For as long as mortals can remember, the East has
been ruled by dragons. Not winged, four-legged drakes,
but unique, serpent-like creatures of immense power
and divine blood. It has been so since the fall of the
Numarii Empire, and even longer still, before the mortal
Teng dared to meddle in the affairs of divine beasts.

Recently, however, the ambitions of a scholar-turnedrebel verged on overthrowing four thousand years of
history. Despite his failure, the tremors he caused were
enough to profoundly change the eastern realms of
Satokai forever.

Father of Dynasts

Before speaking of the three storied rulers of Satokai,
we must first account for their progenitor–the legendary
dragon-God Leung, Wyrm of Supremacy, a creature that
clawed its way to divinity through toil and hardship in
an age long forgotten.

In the early days of the universe, Leung descended
upon central Eos and claimed a territory none were
willing to contest. Among the wild, untamed lands of a
great continent, the serpent dragon built his earthly
empire. Palaces of jade, amber, and ruby rose at the
hands of his many servants, and his power reached
across the nascent planes until all knew the Wyrm of
Supremacy had chosen his seat. He called the land
where he settled Satokai, and named himself the First
Dynast of an Empire that would last for thousands of
years.
The Partition

In contact with the mortals of Eos, as well as many
visiting dignitaries from across the planes, Leung was
struck by a profound yearning. He wished to sire
children, in the way of mortals and divine unions, but
creatures like him did not have that ability–not innately,
at least. As a God, however, he had a certain degree of
control over the laws of the Universe, and so, he began
the process of creating children, imposing his supreme
will.

The great Vazan was the firstborn, and Leung
realized a fraction of his divinity had passed on to
him. This made Leung rejoice, despite a certain
loss of station. The sacrifice of oneself in service
of the generations to come is the greatest gift to life,
he proclaimed, and the twin dragonesses Fulgurai
and Xiyun came next.

Despite having the drive to sire more, three
children were all he managed before the Divine
Domain of Supremacy became too diluted to suffer
another partition–in fact, Fulgurai and Xiyun were
already considerably weaker than their elder brother.
Supremacy, after all, is a crown fit for one head only.
Dividing it was a great affront to the nature of the
domain–one that would eventually exact a great toll
upon its heirs.
Original PDF page 181

Original PDF page 182

Leung’s Folly

Like most other Gods, Leung was called to sacrifice
himself in the final charge of the War of Eternity, giving
his life so the Universe might live. The remaining
fraction of Supremacy was expected to go to his three
children, who would further his legacy into the godless
ages to come. It wasn’t quite so: Supremacy, desirous of
unity by nature, accumulated in Vazan, who already
bore a larger fraction of it. The result was that Vazan
wielded about half of Supremacy, and the twins wielded
one-fourth each. A state of affairs that begets
resentment, as any scholar of divinity will tell you.

From “To See a God”, Introduction
Divine Domains have a will of their own. They are
capricious laws of reality, born in an age when the rules of
the Universe were still forming. These aspects of existence
strive for their own satisfaction and most often do so
against one another. The Gods of Old incarnated these
domains, and indeed carried them with great responsibility,
but the domains themselves were a separate thing from the
personalities and lives of their wielders.

When the Gods sacrificed themselves to keep Eternity at
bay, their domains were fractured, becoming Threads of
Dvinity and passing unto lesser vessels–unworthy, all. In
time, even the most prepared Wielders, such as the Teng
leaders and the very children of the Gods, saw their Divine
Domains turn against them.

– Myriel Genra, Threadseeker of Divinity

Vazan’s Subjugation

Vazan’s primacy in the Dark Years that followed the
Fall of the Gods lasted for about half a century, during
which he alone determined the fate of Satokai. His
twins were relegated to fringe regional rulers, endowed
with great riches and luxury, but with little true power.

Xiyun and Fulgurai thus made a pact with the rulers
of the nascent Numarii empire, a Teng civilization led
by two Wielders of Divinity; heirs to the powers of the
Gods, much like Vazan himself.

The warrior-king Agnar, who carried the Thread of
Might, overthrew the powerful Supremacy of Vazan
with the sneaky help of the twin dragonesses. In
exchange for their help, Xiyun and Fulgurai were
named viceroys of Satokai, though Agnar refused to
bestow upon them their brother’s divinity.

Vazan was captured and humiliated at first, paraded
in arcane chains around Numar, but then slowly
rehabilitated by Agnar, who respected the power of the
dragon and sought to use him in further conquests. As it
turned out, Vazan was fairly pliable to this objective:
with the Numarii and his own sisters against him, he
harbored little hope of regaining Satokai’s throne.
Through centuries of war, Vazan became a highly
respected and effective commander in the Teng
empire’s legions, even leading incursions into the

jungles of Kratakan, where resistance against Numar
was strongest. Tales of the combined power of Might
and Supremacy echoed throughout the cosmos, striking
fear in the hearts of other conquerors of the Second
Age.

From “Examinations”, Section 20-C
The fall of the Numarii empire was something Vazan surely
expected, though it took a very long time. He expected it
because, at the heart of the empire, was another Wielder of
Divinity: Queen Persephone, wife of Agnar. While her
husband bore the Thread of Might, her charge was the
Thread of Dusk, one of the terrible aspects of Nyx, Goddess
of Night. It was a poisoned shard of divinity, in truth,
something no mortal–not even the powerful Teng–could
hope to bear without being consumed. The same could be
said of Might, of course, which led her husband to wage
war without end…

Persephone and Agnar turned against each other after
three thousand years of rule, with the Queen attempting to
relieve the conqueror from his belligerent Thread, so that
she may have the company of her consort in her dolorous
task. The result of that confrontation was the destruction of
Numar, the death of an entire civilization, and most
importantly for our study of Satokai… the liberation of
Vazan.

–Guilliam Fallow, Threadmaster of Arcana & Science

The Ruby Restoration

Vazan was very far from Numar when the Empire fell,
for he knew it would happen eventually. Thousands of
years of sitting out war councils, royal proclamations,
and ceremonial acts finally paid off: anarchy had come,
and the legions under his command were unscathed.
Adding to that, since he was the highest-ranking general
of the Numarii army, many powerful Teng joined him as
he sailed them across the Kelmarine Sea, promising his
followers wealth and stability in Satokai. Fulgurai and
Xiyun, viceroys of a defunct empire and severely
outnumbered, sent out a peace missive to their longestranged brother, and the three dragons met within the
Supreme Palace, Leung’s luxurious seat in the Cloud
Mountains of northern Satokai.

None except them can claim to know what was
discussed there, far above the clouds, but restraint in
the exercise of power was the result. Vazan exacted no
terrible vengeance–he even granted Xiyun and Fulgurai
vast lands in the restored Ruby Dynasty, while
adjudicating himself a comparatively modest seat in
Asiore, the Crimson Isle. All the restitution demanded
by Vazan was taxes, which Xiyun and Fulgurai were
made to pay in copious amounts. The isle prospered to
become one of the wealthiest realms in the material
plane, and Satokai at large followed suit, profiting from
uncommon stability within the chaotic context of the
Second Age.

Original PDF page 183

Rise of the Yokai
Court Mystic Ge Hong

Despite the wealth, artistry, and power that were
amassed in Satokai after the Ruby Restoration,
something baleful was brewing behind the scenes. The
Soul River, a great flux of water that ran through the
center of Satokai, became spiritually and arcanely
charged. Strange and sometimes malicious spirits
emerged in villages and cities, beings of potent emotion
