that fed off intense feelings. Yokai, they were called, and
soon enough, restraint of passion was imposed as law,
in an attempt to curb their growing numbers. Many
Teng of Numar emigrated over the centuries, unwilling
to submit their temperament to the censorship of the
dragon dynasts.

Despite the concerted effort against them, the Yokai
threat remained a permanent feature of the land, and
their spiritual weight was enough to corrupt the
overlapping ethereal dimension, creating a spiritual
energy pit called the Narak, a sort of hell for souls.

The Origin of the Narak

The mysterious afflictions of Satokai were addressed
with policies that tackled the symptoms, but no efforts
were made to investigate their source. This was for the
very simple reason that Vazan, Fulgurai, and Xiyun
already knew why it was happening. It was the Divine
Domain of Supremacy rebelling against the alliance
enacted by its wielders, a treaty of compromise and
distribution of power that was directly anathema to
Leung’s divinity. Though a powerful warlord under
Agnar, Vazan was a temperate, wise, and regal creature,
unwilling to slay his little sisters, even after their
betrayal.

Suffice it to say, the dragon dynasts were unwilling to
address the source of the afflictions, and so, they
directed vast resources to alleviate its symptoms. This
meant curbing the expansion and intensity of the
Narak’s spiritual corruption, of course, but also finding
remedies to the brutal psychic and physical devastation
Supremacy was unleashing upon them. Each of the
dynasts surrounded themselves with a cohort of healers
and mages, charged with the tremendous responsibility
of keeping them in good health and preserving their
noble image in front of the populace.

Of all the learned men and women who assisted the
dynasts during this time, the best was undoubtedly Ge
Hong. He was a half-Teng, half-human mystic arts
scholar proficient in many esoteric disciplines, including
wizardry, alchemy, and Focus manipulation. His
contemporaries described him as extremely wise for his
youth and bearing a mind as sharp as dwarven steel.
The Court Mystic was so effective that he treated all
three Divine Dragons.

Though he was repeatedly discouraged from doing
so, Ge Hong pursued research on the origin of the
dynasts’ illness, and, since he was a man of many
interests, on the Narak as well. It didn’t take him long to
realize the two issues were related. The nature of
Supremacy was not public knowledge, and thus, he was
missing a key piece of the puzzle. To understand why
their own godly powers were killing his masters, he
began a new vector of research, one looking into divinity
itself. The ironic thing is, everything he did, he did to
serve the dragons better…

Being the foremost mystic of the three courts of
Satokai, research voyages and expensive ingredients
were easy to purvey, so long as he came up with a good
excuse for their necessity–which he always did. Though
learning the hermetic affairs of immortals was a difficult
task, Ge Hong persevered, and finally came to
understand the arcane contamination of the Soul River,
the Yokai, the Narak, and the sickness afflicting the
divine dragons as one singular issue.

From “Published Memoirs of the Silver
Dragon”, page 167
It was my sister, Syara, who gave Ge Hong the final clue
that brought him to villainy. Moved by his account of the
dynasts’ suffering, she told him the tale of the Wyrm of
Supremacy’s ascension, revealing the nature of his divinity,
hoping he could use it to further his healing prowess. Just
another chapter in her long string of careless acts, though
few turned out to be as destructive as this one. The halfteng mystic went on to murder a God and motivate two
others into blasphemy, as he considered that the only way
to truly heal the cosmic wound of the Narak was to unify
Supremacy forevermore. In his view, it was the immortals’
lack of judiciousness that brought forth the corruption of
Satokai.

One could argue that Ge Hong was right. But he himself
lacked judiciousness, as demonstrated by the absurd notion
that he could be the one to wield unified Supremacy.

–Garas Dawnscale, Threadmaster of Exploration

Original PDF page 184

Into the Narak

Ge Hong determined that the only way to heal Satokai
was to unify Supremacy once more. Other methods of
containing the corruption were merely palliative, even if
the dynasts couldn’t see that. In an act of pride that was
slowly becoming characteristic of mortalkind, he
decided that he should be the one to wield Supremacy.
He was the only one who both understood it and was
willing to mend it, after all.

One fateful night, he gathered his most precious
instruments, his research scrolls, and his expensive
magical components and fled Asiore. He travelled to the
Supreme Palace, former seat of Leung and the very
place where the Ruby Restoration was negotiated, for
he suspected it to be the root of all corruption. And
indeed, he found a festering wound in the planar fabric
of the material plane, a seeping portal into the Narak
that was overcome with wailing Yokai. He appeased
them using his necromantic knowledge and ventured
inside, becoming the first living being to bear witness to
the enormity of the wounded ethereal realm.

An Eastern Solution

The Yokai of the Narak were not outright hostile to Ge
Hong, which was strange to him. He attributed it to his
great arcane prowess, when it was in fact his immense
pride that compelled the Yokai to leave him alone;
emotion is power in the Narak, and few are the feelings
that can match the dominance of a proud soul. He spoke
to the many spirits therein and became convinced of his
purpose: to save his land, Supremacy had to be whole
again.

Even with their powers split and weakened by divine
sickness, few creatures in Eos stood a chance against
the Divine Dragons of the East, Dynasts of Satokai, and
children of Leung. To do what had to be done, he
required power beyond his capabilities, perhaps beyond
the capability of any mortal. He considered requesting
the aid of Nemeq, the Divine Lion, ruler of the nation of
Kahar, or perhaps the Teng king of Argencia, beyond
the material plane… but no. It wouldn’t do. This was an
eastern problem, and it would be solved by an eastern
hero, lest they become a colony again, like in the days of
Numar.

The Oni Emperor

The neglect suffered by Supremacy was exploitable, Ge
Hong found. The supernatural powers of the Yokai were
born of the wailing domain; it was raging against its
wielders, causing them all sorts of trouble. Perhaps a
credible promise of unification would be enough to
become Supremacy’s champion, and that could mean
the aid of the Yokai. Better yet, in a confrontation, the
powers of Supremacy might refuse to aid the dynasts.

Ge Hong’s theories were dead on, as it turned out. To
acquire power, he performed a ritual to separate his
soul from his body and bound it to the Narak, effectively
becoming a Yokai. Beaming with ego, the rageful spirits
created by Supremacy were drawn to him like moths to

a light, and Ge Hong became a creature called the Oni
Emperor, the first true sovereign of the Narak. In this
form, he terrorized Satokai, learning to control the
hordes of spirits to his will. The inhabitants of the land
came to call him Pride, on occasion; it was his immense
arrogance that allowed him such power, after all. The
crisis in the east deepened as it became evident that the
serpent dynasts were ill-equipped to defeat him,
especially in the absence of their most accomplished
court Mystic.

The Right Tool

Ge Hong’s nightly raids had a purpose beyond that of
war: he was searching for the components to craft a
weapon. Despite his newfound might and longcultivated vanity, he harbored no illusions about his
chances in a direct confrontation with any of Leung’s
children. Supremacy would–hopefully–be on his side,
but that was hardly enough for a ghost to beat a divine
dragon. After decades of raids and war, the ghostflame
forges of the Narak produced their final work: a weapon
capable of slaying a God.

The ingredients were three: first, a blade of living
metal, sharp and conductive of arcane energy unlike any
other. Second, a hollow handle that doubled as a potent
arcane receptacle of Numari steel, capable of syphoning
even the most powerful essences. Finally, a sheath of
dragon scales, to contain the weapon in a structure
resilient enough for the Oni Emperor’s plans. The
resulting wakizashi was dubbed Vazan’s Terror, a
foreboding name that was, in fact, more of a promise
than anything else.
The Siege of Asiore

The spectral hordes of the Oni Emperor fell upon the
Crimson Isle in the year 2444 B.V. As graceful shinobi
fended off sadistic yokai all across the island, Ge Hong
met his former liege in the Ruby Throne. An eradefining battle ensued, in which man–or rather, Yokai–
prevailed against the dragon. Unbeknownst to the victor
of the battle, however, there was a third fighter waiting
in the wings. From the shadows of the palace, keen-eyed
Master of Spies Kaltoven, the greatest soldier of
Vazan’s inner circle, witnessed the confrontation in
perfect silence.

pages.name: p. 185 - “Vazan was quite sure Ge couldn’t pose a serious threat,

text.content:
Original PDF page 185

“Vazan was quite sure Ge couldn’t pose a serious threat,

especially alone, but the moment the Oni Emperor drew

steel, I understood… he was ready for the enormity of

his quest. Counting on Vazan’s arrogance, he managed

to stab into him, piercing through the scales with his

mystical weapon. As a retort, he was slashed twice in

quick succession, thrown against a pillar with enough

force to break it, and blasted with Vazan’s crimson

flames. Ge rose from the wreck, visibly battered by the

onslaught… but grinning.

Those were the final moments of the Crimson

Dynast. From the wound inflicted by the master healer’s

blade, Supremacy began spilling out in strands of

spectral silver. The Oni Emperor lifted his wakizashi far

above his head, and the flow of power came to him.

There was much fight left in Vazan, but in truth, that

first thrust had spelled his doom. I had to bide my time

for the right opportunity to strike: a single blow from

either combatant would vaporize me. That much I

knew.”

–Crimson Shogun Kaltoven

Vazan’s Fate

Vazan fell from the sky, the Oni Emperor pushing down
against the dragon with the power of Supremacy now
connected to his blade. The Crimson Dynast was so
spiritually wounded he could no longer move, and Ge
Hong’s blade started extracting the dragon’s divinity at
a violent pace. It required the Oni Emperor’s complete
focus, and in that moment, Master Shinobi Kaltoven
sprang from the shadows, stabbing his kunai into the
Oni Emperor’s back. Such was the blow of the magical
weapons, enchanted to banish Yokai, the Emperor was
momentarily expunged from reality, leaving the
ritual incomplete and Vazan’s Terror behind.

The storied dragon convulsed beneath his savior,
dozens of arcane perforations riddling his scaly body,
and despite Kaltoven’s extraordinary efforts, Vazan
perished. Before the Oni Emperor could return, the
Master Shinobi took the imbued blade that was left
behind and, using its power, expelled the Yokai from the
palace, ending the invasion.

In the ruins of Vazan’s Seat, Kaltoven was
provisionally hailed as Shogun of Asiore by the
surviving members of the dragon’s court. They were
deathly afraid of the Oni Emperor, and it seemed only
the Master of Spies could mount meaningful resistance
should another incursion come. Vazan’s Terror
remained with him–now the foremost symbol of
dynastic legitimacy in Asiore.
A Shade in the Narak

Vazan’s death was nothing short of torture, messy and
ritualistic. All that agony coalesced within the Narak,
and in a cruel twist of fate, a part of Vazan became a
terrible spectral yokai, a manifestation of the dynast’s
tortured soul. This monster came to be known as the
Lord of Anguish, a broken entity of negative emotion,
given form by the shards of the Divine Domain of
Supremacy that remained lodged within the Dynast at
the time of his death.

Driven by a terrible fear of Yokai and all things
spectral, the Lord of Anguish escaped from its place of
origin and began to wander aimlessly through the
ethereal, shuddering in distress. If only that were all, its
existence would not be such a blight… but the Lord of
Anguish can take physical form, in the way of the
mightiest incorporeal undead, and its unendurable
sorrow leads him to indiscriminate destruction.

pages.name: p. 186 - A Blight Across the Universe

text.content:
Original PDF page 186

A Blight Across the Universe

Anyone remotely interested in history or mythology has
heard tell of a terrible serpentine monster, a deeply
mysterious entity, which comes out of nowhere, wreaks
absolute devastation, and disappears into the ethereal
dimension once more, only to strike again a century
later somewhere very, very far away. Even when it is
killed by great heroes or massive armies, it supposedly
always returns. Its attacks are so sporadic and
disorganized that its very existence is, to this day,
considered folk superstition in most scholarly circles.

That theory has been disproven in recent times,
however, as the ghost of Vazan attacked Aurel, the
massive capital of the Kingdom of Iskendar, only two
hundred years ago. Part of its skeletal remains still
decorate the royal palace, and the assault was recorded
with rigorous detail by the archivists of the kingdom. It
killed over one thousand before it wandered towards the
capital’s port, where the Cobalt Navy held anchor. The
Lord of Anguish was then shot down from the sky by
deafening cannonades.
The Latest Apparition

Only seven years ago, the Lord of Anguish fell upon
Galivan’s Reach, the famous astral port orbiting the City
of Strings itself. The creature was blasted into oblivion
by the Order of War under the command of Myrrha
Kal’Oshar, but not before claiming hundreds of lives
and causing unquantifiable damage to the merchant
town. This attack prompted the City of Strings to
conduct a thorough investigation into the nature of this
spectral behemoth, which yielded fascinating results.
The research papers published about this being were
foundational to the modern study of divinity, its
potential for corruption, and its intrinsic volition beyond
that of its wielders.

As far as practical conclusions go, there were a few:
first, that the Lord of Anguish was very likely a Yokai of
Ruby Emperor Vazan. Second, that the Lord of Anguish
was, like all Yokai, drawn to accumulations of negative
emotion. When it attacked Aurel, the 150th anniversary
of the Battle of Roncev was being commemorated in the
great capital. Speeches by noblemen, generals, and
inquisitors were reminding the populace of great losses,
heroic triumphs, and generally inciting righteous hate
against the northern rebels. These… stirring topics
evidently acted as a flare to attract the monster.

In Galivan’s Reach, Anguish was also attracted by a
great accumulation of emotional energy, though it
seems it was artificially generated; a lure of sorts, in the
form of a ritual, explicitly tuned to attract the Lord of
Anguish. Those who used the creature to their own
ends were surely hoping for the City of Strings’
downfall. The fact that its existence can be exploited is
proof enough that finding a permanent resolution to its
menace is of paramount importance.

Defeating Anguish

If the theories linking the Lord of Anguish to Vazan are
as true as they are convincing, the question of putting
the Lord of Anguish down is… complicated. A demigod
is not easily felled, or even the shade of one, for that
matter. Fallow’s calculations posit that, unless Anguish
is assassinated with a void weapon, its essence will
inevitably return to the Narak to be reincarnated. Since
the use of void weapons is prohibited by the laws of the
Nephilim and the City of Strings, there remains only
one other option…

The Oni Emperor’s ritual must be completed,
imprisoning what remains of the Dragon Dynast’s soul
and divinity within Kaltoven’s wakizashi, to be wielded
by others unto eternity. This artifact, forged for that sole
purpose, is perhaps the only one that can measure up to
the task of imprisoning the draconic demigod of
Supremacy. The issue is that it requires Kaltoven’s
collaboration, which in turn requires his trust–a very
difficult thing to obtain. In the meantime, the Lord of
Anguish slaughters thousands, until it can be located
and stopped by the efforts of the Universe’s greatest
heroes. Some have posited that stealing the weapon
from Kaltoven and returning it upon the completion of
the ritual wouldn’t be so terrible a crime, if the
diplomatic way fails…

Theseus’ Notes on Legendary Foes
Good old Vazan, the firstborn of Leung. Always so willful, so
organized and full of cunning. Even though I did hope the
Oni Emperor’s brutal strike would have ridden us of this
enemy, it seems some part of him is what is generating the
monster known as the Lord of Anguish. In truth, I am torn,
my dear Eternity. On one hand, this psychotic beast
periodically lays waste to great cities, and we both know
what great cities really are–the breeding grounds of noble
heroes and other inconveniences. On the other hand, it
builds trust and unity between the planeswalking powers
that, sooner or later, end up defeating each of its
reincarnations. Should we kill it, permanently with your
essence, or let it continue its rampage? What is most
detrimental to the defenders of the sickening light? I’ll defer
to your judgement, of course, dear Eternity.

But… If we were to murder it, stealth is of the utmost
importance. Anguish’s obsessive soul needs a target, and
once it finds it, it obliterates it with little difficulty and
unbridled brutality. We must not give it one; its blindsight
range is considerable, but not infinite, and, unlike with
Vazan’s divine draconic form, it does not benefit from any
kind of divine sight to see through illusions. Smoke and
mirrors, my dear Eternity. Our work always boils down to
that, doesn’t it?

Original PDF page 187

Vazan’s Terror

Weapon (Wakizashi), Relic (requires attunement)

A storied weapon, Vazan’s Terror is a short blade of
living metal, adorned with a handle of Numari steel and
a sheath of dragonscale. Moreover, it was cast in
ghostflame, a soul-eating fire that imbues the few metals
that resist it with the concentrated potential of
mortalkind. This mythological craftsmanship was
required in order to contain and channel the might of
Supremacy itself. Such as it is, Vazan’s Terror is one of
the few Relics in the Universe that can potentially
ascend a mortal into functional godhood, should it
contain all shards of Supremacy.

Attunement. Only a creature that embodies the ideals
of Supremacy can attune to Vazan’s Terror. As a Relic,
Vazan’s Terror requires two attunement slots instead of
one. To attempt attunement, a creature must have a
total character level of 20 or more, or be of CR 20 or
higher. When attempting attunement, a creature must
make a DC 25 Charisma saving throw. Alternatively, it
can make a DC 30 saving throw using another ability
score of its choice, representing its Supremacy in a
different domain. On a success, they become attuned to
Vazan’s Terror.

Unworthiness. If the divinity within the blade
determines its wielder unworthy of Supremacy, it must
succeed on a DC 30 Charisma saving throw whenever it
finishes a long rest or take 105 (6d6 x 5) force damage
and be forcibly unattuned from the artifact. Factors that
lead to unworthiness include cowardice, sharing power,
allowing treason to go unpunished, and other acts
uncharacteristic of an emperor, at the DM’s discretion.
Failure in this saving throw cannot be overturned with
the use of a Legendary Resistance.

Supreme Blade. You have a +5 bonus to hit and
damage rolls when you attack with this magic weapon.
In addition, Vazan’s Terror deals an additional 14
(3d10) soul damage on a hit.

Bane of Dragons Great & Small. When you hit a
Dragon that is a Legendary Foe with an attack made
with Vazan’s Terror for the first time in a round of
combat, they lose 1 Legendary Point. If you hit a Dragon
that is not a Legendary Foe, you deal an additional 50
points of soul damage instead.

Supremacy. While attuned to Vazan’s Terror, you have
a +10 bonus to ability checks you roll as part of a
contest. Additionally, you are immune to the charmed
and frightened conditions, as well as spells and magical
effects that would compel your behavior, such as the
Command and Dominate Person spells.

Dynastic Power. While attuned to Vazan’s Terror, roll
1d4 when you finish a long rest to determine its
benefits, which last until you must roll again.

1 - Divine Awareness: You gain truesight out to 60 feet.
Additionally, you know when a lie is spoken in your
presence.

2 - Divine Reflection: You have a +2 bonus to your AC.
Any time you are targeted by a magic missile spell, a
line spell, or a spell that requires a ranged attack roll,
roll 1d6. On a 1 to 5, you are unaffected by the spell. On
a 6, you are unaffected, and the effect is reflected back
at the caster as though it originated from you, turning
the caster into the target.

3 - Serpent’s Might: Choose one ability score: between
Strength, Dexterity, or Constitution. That score
increases by 10, to a maximum of 30, until your next
long rest.

4 - Dynast’s Mind: Choose one ability score between
Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma. That score
increases by 10, to a maximum of 30, until your next
long rest.

Ascension. Vazan’s Terror is designed for much
greater power than it currently holds, with only one
quarter of the Divine Domain of Supremacy contained
within. While Vazan’s Terror is attuned to a worthy
wielder, it automatically absorbs any unclaimed Shards
of Supremacy within 15 feet of it. The Lord of Anguish
possesses one quarter of Supremacy, while the Wyrm of
Blasphemy contains the missing half. For every
additional quarter of Supremacy absorbed, Vazan’s
Terror grows in power, as detailed in the following
milestones:

50%: You roll twice instead of once to determine the
effects granted by the Dynastic Power trait, rerolling
duplicates.

75%: The extra force damage dealt by Vazan’s Terror
becomes 27 (5d10), and you can choose the benefits of
the Dynastic Power trait instead of rolling.

100%: At full power, the effects of the Dynastic Power
trait improve, and you are under the effect of all four
benefits simultaneously. Divine Awareness now grants
truesight out to 120 feet and immunity to psychic
damage. Divine Reflection now grants a +5 bonus to
AC, and affected spells are reflected on a roll of 4 to 6.
Serpent’s Might and Dynast’s Mind now each increase
two ability scores of your choice by 10, to a maximum of
30, instead of one. Through this blade, you are a true
Wielder of Divinity.

Original PDF page 188

The Lord of Anguish

Gargantuan Dragon/Undead, chaotic neutral

Armor Class 25 (natural armor)

Hit Points 1850 (100d20 + 800)

Speed 60 ft., fly 150 ft. (hover)

STR
DEX
CON
INT
WIS
CHA

33 (+11)
18 (+4)
26 (+8)
5 (-3)
20 (+5)
22 (+6)

Saving Throws Str +21, Con +18, Wis +15, Cha +16
Skills Athletics +21
Damage Resistances bludgeoning, cold, fire, lightning,
piercing, slashing
Damage Immunities necrotic, poison; bludgeoning, piercing,
and slashing from nonmagical attacks
Condition Immunities poisoned, charmed, deafened,
exhaustion, grappled, paralyzed, petrified, prone, stunned
Senses blindsight 500 ft. (blind beyond this range), passive
Perception 15
Recommended Level 22nd - 23rd

Proficiency Bonus +10

Limited Magic Immunity. The Lord of Anguish cannot be
affected or detected by spells cast at 3rd level or lower unless
it wishes to be. It has advantage on saving throws against
other spells and magical effects.

Dragon/Undead. The Lord of Anguish is both a dragon and
an undead. Spells and effects that target these creature types
also affect the Lord of Anguish.

Semi-Ethereal. The Lord of Anguish is partly ethereal. It can
move through other creatures and objects as if they were
difficult terrain. It takes 22 (4d10) force damage if it ends its
turn inside an object.

Reconstitution. Unless its soul is imprisoned within a
powerful artifact or creature, the Lord of Anguish gains a
new body 1d10 years after being destroyed, regaining all its
hit points and becoming active again. The new body appears
in a random location in the Ethereal plane.

Gargantuan Strikes. The Lord of Anguish’s attacks are
magical. When the Lord of Anguish hits a target with an
attack, creatures and objects within 15 feet of the target are
also targeted by that attack. The attack roll remains the same
for all targets.

Psychic Scar. The Lord of Anguish has disadvantage on
saving throws against being frightened. If it takes 50 or more

damage in a single instance, it becomes frightened of its
source for 1 minute or until the source is no longer within its
blindsight range. The Lord of Anguish can willingly move
towards the source of its fear while it is frightened, but must
expend 2 feet of movement for every foot traveled.

Smother Terror. If the Lord of Anguish is frightened of a
creature, its attacks deal an additional 30 psychic damage to
that creature. While frightened, the Lord of Anguish will
attempt to kill or swallow the source of its fear. If it succeeds,
the frightened condition ends for the Lord of Anguish. If the
Lord of Anguish is frightened of more than one source, it will
target the nearest creature or object it is frightened of.

Legendary Foe

The Lord of Anguish has 4 Legendary Points (LP). It can use
these points to fuel certain abilities. Once it uses a feature or
action that consumes a Legendary Point, it cannot use that
same ability again until the start of its next turn. The Lord of
Anguish regains spent Legendary Points at the start of its
turn.

Legendary Resistance. When the Lord of Anguish fails a
saving throw, it can expend 1 Legendary Point to succeed
instead.

Legendary Endurance. When the Lord of Anguish takes
damage, it can expend 1 Legendary Point to reduce the
damage taken by 100.

Cleanse Effect. If the Lord of Anguish starts its turn affected
by a condition or magical effect of 7th level or lower, it can
expend 1 Legendary Point to end the effect. If the effect or
condition is of 8th or 9th level, or from a Mythical Technique,
the Lord of Anguish must expend 2 Legendary Points to end
that effect. Effects originating from a 10th-level spell cannot
be cleansed in this way.

Phase Transition

The creature’s outer shell turns to black ash and dissipates in the
wind, revealing a powerful red serpent underneath, two brilliant
ruby eyes where the folds of its arm-covered maw end. It writhes
in agony, yet its crimson gaze reveals a deadlier foe than before.

If the Lord of Anguish is reduced to 500 hit points or fewer, it
is cleansed of all harmful conditions, regains 2 Legendary
Points and gains the following additional traits.

Anguish Eternal. The Lord of Anguish is frightened of all
creatures and objects it can see.

Rage of the Dynast. The Lord of Anguish gains a +5 bonus to
hit with all its attacks and can make an additional Body Slam
as part of its Multiattack action.

Original PDF page 189

Actions
Bonus Actions

Multiattack. The Lord of Anguish uses its Aura of Negative
Emotion. It then makes three attacks: one with its ArmCovered Maw and two with its Body Slam.

Aura of Negative Emotion. Creatures within 60 feet of the
Lord of Anguish must succeed on a DC 20 Wisdom saving
throw or take 21 (6d6) psychic damage and be affected by a
wave of negative emotion. Roll 1d6 to determine the effect.

1 - Anguish: The creature is frightened of the Lord of Anguish
until the end of its next turn.

2 - Loathing: Until the end of its next turn, the creature’s
attacks have a +5 to hit and damage rolls, but it becomes
vulnerable to all damage for the same duration.

3 - Sloth: The creature’s speed is halved, and it can only take
an action or a bonus action on its turn, but not both, until
the end of its next turn.

4 - Envy: The creature must spend its next turn attempting to
steal an object it considers precious that is currently in the
possession of a creature within 120 feet. If there are no such
objects or it fails to do so, it takes 35 (10d6) psychic
damage.

5 - Sorrow: The creature is taken by a deep melancholy. It
cannot regain hit points or be affected by beneficial magical
effects such as haste or greater restoration until the end of its
next turn.

6 - Shame: The creature’s speed becomes 0, and it cannot
speak or communicate in any way until the end of its next
turn. While affected by shame, it is unable to perform verbal
components for spells.

If the Lord of Anguish is frightened, a creature affected by this
aura must roll twice on the table of effects instead of once,
re-rolling duplicates.

Arm-Covered Maw. Melee Weapon Attack: +21 to hit, reach 10
ft., one target. Hit 76 (10d12 + 11) bludgeoning damage, and
the target is grappled and restrained (escape DC 29). If
damage from this attack reduces a creature to 0 hit points, it
is torn apart and swallowed by the Lord of Anguish,
destroying its remains. The Lord of Anguish can have
multiple creatures grappled in this way at a time.

Body Slam (-5). Melee Weapon Attack: +16 to hit, reach 20 ft.,
one target. Hit 32 (4d12 + 6) bludgeoning damage.

Beam of Negative Emotion. The Lord of Anguish exhales a
beam of devastating psychic energy in a 1,000-foot-long line
that is 20 feet wide. Creatures within that area must make a
DC 26 Dexterity saving throw, taking 65 (10d12) psychic
damage and 65 (10d12) Dark damage on a failed save, or
half as much on a successful one. Creatures that fail this
saving throw are subjected to a random effect from the Aura
of Negative Emotion list.

Ghost. The Lord of Anguish shifts into the ethereal plane
until the start of its next turn. Any objects it is carrying and
creatures it is grappling (swallowed or otherwise) shift with
it.

Body Slam (Bonus Action). The Lord of Anguish makes a Body
Slam attack.

Swallow (Costs 1 LP). The Lord of Anguish expends 1
Legendary Point to swallow all Huge or smaller creatures it is
currently grappling. A swallowed creature is blinded and
restrained; it has total cover against attacks and other effects
outside the Lord of Anguish, and it takes 45 (10d8) Dark
damage at the start of each of the Lord of Anguish’s turns. A
swallowed creature ignores the Lord of Anguish’s damage
resistances and has a +5 to hit and damage rolls against it.

If the Lord of Anguish takes 100 damage or more in a single
turn from a creature inside it, it regurgitates all swallowed
creatures, which fall prone in unoccupied spaces within 10
feet of the mouth. If the Lord of Anguish dies, its body
disintegrates, and all swallowed creatures fall prone in
unoccupied spaces within 50 feet of the Lord of Anguish’s
remains.

Extra Actions

The Lord of Anguish can take extra actions outside of its turn by
expending Legendary Points, choosing from the options below.
Only one extra action can be used at a time and only at the end
of another creature’s turn.

Onslaught (Costs 1 LP). The Lord of Anguish makes two Body
Slam attacks.

Glide (Costs 1 LP). The Lord of Anguish moves up to its
maximum speed in a direction of its choosing.

Brood (Costs 1 LP). The Lord of Anguish becomes frightened
of a creature it can see within 600 feet of it.

Arm-Covered Maw (Costs 2 LP). The Lord of Anguish makes
one Arm-Covered Maw attack.

Beam (Costs 2 LP). The Lord of Anguish uses its Beam of
Negative Emotion.

Swallow (Costs 2 LP). The Lord of Anguish uses its Swallow
bonus action.

Pulse of Anguish (Costs 4 LP). The Lord of Anguish’s body
pulses with emotional energy, exuding crippling psychic
waves in a 500-foot area around it. Creatures within that area
must succeed on a DC 25 Wisdom saving throw or take 35
(10d6) psychic damage and be blinded, deafened, frightened,
and incapacitated for one minute as they begin to weep black
tears. Affected creatures can repeat the saving throw at the
end of each of their turns, ending the conditions on
themselves on a success. A casting of Greater Restoration or
similar magic, such as a paladin’s Cleansing Touch, ends
this effect. Swallowed creatures are not affected by this
action.

Original PDF page 190

DM Notes

The Lord of Anguish is one of our more straightforward
Legendary Foes, with mechanics that are easy to run
but still create a memorable battle. Its core gameplay
revolves around the frightened condition; to this broken
shade of a Dynast, being scared is not a bug, but a
feature. Here are some notes about the stat sheet and
how to run it.

Inflicting Anguish

The appropriately named Lord of Anguish is designed
to be a psychological terror on the battlefield, dipping in
and out of the ethereal plane while swallowing party
members one by one and inflicting a myriad of bane-like
effects. Lean into this! The battle against the dark
serpent should feel like climbing out of a pit of negative
emotion. But before climbing out, one must fall in, and
Anguish applies such a sheer amount of harmful
conditions that even the most abjuration and trumpcard-loaded parties will struggle not to fall for at least a
couple.

It helps to portray the Lord of Anguish not as
malicious but as a sad victim of unendurable psychic
fracture. It can’t help but be afraid of everything, and it
also can’t help but lay waste to its surroundings with its
massive yet uncontrolled power.
Obsession

Even though narratively, Anguish is terrified of
everything at all times, it mechanically focuses most of
its dread on one or two targets—those it is frightened of.
In terms of power, being frightened is actually
convenient for the Lord of Anguish: the movement
restriction is weaker on it, and it grants a considerable
damage boost thanks to its Smother Terror trait.

Anguish becomes frightened of the latest creature
that damaged it significantly. Your players might use
this to their advantage–let them! The Lord of Anguish
can also become frightened of a creature willingly with
its Brood extra action, which you can use in a pinch if
the encounter would benefit from it.

Eat your Demons

The Lord of Anguish’s most powerful ability is its ArmCovered Maw attack. With its Gargantuan Strikes
feature, it can hit multiple characters with a single bite
attack and swallow them before its turn ends. The
swallow mechanic is much more interactive than your
typical swallow; though the players will take a
considerable amount of damage while inside the Lord of
Anguish, they will also deal more damage, bypassing
resistances and gaining a bonus to hit against their foe.

Though being eaten is distressing, it is actually better
to be inside the Lord of Anguish than outside, since a
swallowed creature cannot be targeted by additional
attacks. Do bear in mind that the damage taken from
the Swallow is Dark damage, a legendary damage type
that reduces a creature’s maximum hit points.

Vazan, Unleashed

When the Lord of Anguish sheds its black shell and
enters its second phase, it becomes much deadlier.
Body Slam attacks that might have been missing highAC characters will now regularly hit, and the frightened
mechanic no longer allows characters to manipulate
Anguish’s movement. The confrontation becomes a race
to the death.

Cinematically communicate to your players the
urgency of defeating Vazan as quickly as possible before
they are overwhelmed.

Tiers of Play

The Lord of Anguish is a Paragon-tier Legendary Foe,
meaning that it is meant to be faced by parties between
the 22nd and 23rd level. These parties possess 10thlevel spells and a 2 LP maximum per character.

Legend

While not nearly at the level to face Anguish under
normal circumstances, it is possible for a mechanically
optimized, relic-bearing 20th-level party to emerge
victorious against it if they play intelligently. Heavy
casualties should be expected, though, as the damage
output is exceedingly high, and its emotion-based
abilities will likely drain several members of
the party of their singular Legendary Point
every round.

Having allies, such as powerful NPCs that
can take the brunt of the Lord of Anguish’s attacks,
can be a way to make this creature work for the
Legend-tier of play. As with all Paragon-tier
creatures, however, a party with only 1 LP to
spend often falls short of these foes.

Original PDF page 191

Paragon

With the advent of your players’ first 10th-level spells and
second Legendary Point, the battle becomes a lot more
viable. A spell such as Avatar alone can entirely change
the course of the fight, with how well it works against a
non-strategizing enemy. It is important for you to
remember (and signal to the players) that party missteps
remain potentially fatal. If a character has no means of
teleportation or ethereal travel, for example, a
combination of the Arm-Covered Maw action, Ghost
bonus action and Dismbember extra action can take one
party member out of the fight very fast. All of that can
happen before the end of round 1! This tension is to be

expected from a Legendary Encounter, however, so be
sure to pull no punches.

Ascended

In the final tier of legendary play covered by this book, the
Lord of Anguish will have a hard time keeping up with the
party. We recommend reserving this Legendary
Encounter as a secondary story point in such cases,
though it can be adapted to pose a greater threat if
needed. To do so, we recommend giving the Lord of
Anguish one more Legendary Point, a hit point boost of
about 300, and more importantly, moving up the health
needed for its Phase Transition to occur to the 1000 hit
point mark.

Original PDF page 192

Blasphemy of the Sisters

The demise of Vazan was hard on all of Satokai, but
none were hit worse than Fulgurai and Xiyun, the two
remaining Dragon Dynasts. Connected to him through
the shared divinity of their father, they felt, albeit in a
diminished manner, every second of the Oni Emperor’s
torment. What this trauma led them to do is
unpseakable, even if understandable. Only thirty years
after Vazan became the Lord of Anguish, both of them
would suffer a similar fate, transmuted into a sin-borne
draconic chimera known only as the Wyrm of
Blasphemy. Unlike their brother, however, they
subjected themselves to this transformation willingly.

Before gazing at the darkness of what they became,
let us examine them as they were, in their centuries of
glory.

Fulgurai, Thunder of the East

Gold-scaled Fulgurai was a serpent of rapturous energy,
known for her volatile temper, love of food and
spectacle, as well as the sheer power of her breath
weapon, which she proudly unleashed in every fight she
tangled herself in. She took part in many of the great
battles of the First and Second ages, often without
much justification, and was even known to fight on both
sides of the same war, seeking exotic and powerful
opponents.

As the centuries passed, she grew tired of the thrills
of combat and began seeking amusement elsewhere.
Few places had better venues, theaters, and artisans
than her seat of Fulguraat, capital of the Yungari
steppes she governed. A deepening decadence took hold
of the Thunder of the East, though, and outside the
capital itself, neglect and lawlessness were the rule. In
this state of licentiousness, Fulgurai was met with the
grim news of Vazan’s demise.
Xiyun, the Blood Dynast

Although physically mighty in her own right, greenscaled, bloodshot Xiyun was very unlike her twin sister.
Her breath weapon wasn’t primordial lightning, but
rather a gaseous substance that could change its
composition in accordance with her will: clouds of
paralysis, poison, confusion, and even petrification. Her
interests were different as well, as she demonstrated a
great love of literature, alchemy, and the scholarly arts.
As with most philosophers, the centuries soured her
heart, as bitter realizations about the nature of mortals
and immortals kept accumulating. Near the end of the
Ruby Dynasty, she had a reputation for cruelty and
cynicism to match her sister’s debauchery. While
Fulgurai lost her discipline and restraint, Xiyun lost her
morality.

A Brutal Lesson

Vazan, mightiest of the three dynasts, paid for his own
stagnation with his life when faced with the Oni
Emperor. Though at one point, the twin sisters sought
to destroy their elder brother, his compassion after the
Ruby Restoration brought them to revere him, and miss
him terribly after his demise. Beyond the grief, Fulgurai
and Xiyun were quick to learn the lessons of the
catastrophe. First, that their decadence was a
dangerous weakness, even in an age without
conquerors such as Agnar. Second, that the will of
Supremacy for unity could no longer be ignored.

The first thing they did was thank Kaltoven for his
service and thorough reporting, and demand he turn
over Ge Hong’s wakizashi… which he refused, correctly
reading their intent to absorb the power within. That
power so happened to be the remnant of his master’s
soul, and unlike the Oni Emperor, Kaltoven was nothing
if not loyal. This defiance enraged the Dynasts, but they
didn’t dare move against him, for Vazan’s court and
army were firmly on the side of the Master Shinobi, and
worse yet, he possessed the Oni Emperor’s dragonkilling weapon.

The Wyrm of Blasphemy

With the fall of Vazan, the ascent of the Oni Emperor,
the disobedience of Kaltoven, and the general
discontent of their subjects, Xiyun and Fulgurai came to
understand just how precarious their situation really
was. Drastic measures were not only warranted but
existentially necessary, and so they hatched a plan: they
would fuse and become a single demigod, mightier than
even Vazan, and powerful enough to defeat both the Oni
Emperor and Kaltoven. Moreover, if they achieved the
complete unity of Supremacy, they would mend
Satokai’s ethereal dimension, bringing an end to the
blights of the Narak.

Thus, it was settled. With Xiyun’s tomes of ancient
necromancy and Fulgurai’s raw magical power, they
devised a ritual of fusion to combine their souls and
bodies. The guiding principle of alchemy is that all is
one and one is all; from that maxim is derived the idea
that, with proper technique, you can transmute anything
into anything else. Even if true, practice has often
shown the limits of that philosophy, and the soul fusion
of the dynast twins was no exception. It resulted in an
unholy serpentine draconid that, despite having
elements of both Xiyun and Fulgurai, was entirely
unrecognizable as either of them, so disfiguring was the
process. But worse than its appearance was its
personality, which combined their flaws while keeping
little of their virtues. A temperamental, impulsive, cruel,
and tyrannical creature, herald of a new age of turmoil
for Satokai.

Original PDF page 193

The Reckoning

The Wyrm of Blasphemy first set its sights on Ge Hong,
that treacherous scullion who killed its brother.
Entering the ethereal dimension was no great challenge
for such a resourceful creature, and disarmed of his
wakizashi, the Oni Emperor was no match for the
Wyrm. Ge Hong escaped, abandoning his spectral
palace while the blasphemous dragon cleaved through
his Yokai forces.

The Narak shrank in intensity when Ge Hong
escaped, as well as in size. Proudly, the Wyrm of
Blasphemy proclaimed to have slain the treacherous
mystic and took its place as great ruler of both Yungar
and Daxin, the two mainland provinces of Satokai. The
tyrant markedly worsened life for all its subjects; levies
and taxes were increased, border disputes with
Iskendar resumed after an age of detente, and long-time
servants of the dynasts’ bureaucracy were replaced with
power-hungry daimyos.

The War of Shogun and Wyrm

The relationship between Asiore, the imperial island,
and the rest of Satokai soured very quickly after the soul
fusion. The Ruby Court declared Kaltoven as the
foremost ruler of the east, while the Wyrm, of course,
crowned itself. No troops have been fielded to battle yet,
no bloody skirmishes fought, and no formal declaration
of enmity has been proclaimed by either side… but spies
and assassins are already plying their trade, laying the
groundwork for a more catastrophic confrontation.

In this potential war, the Wyrm side has the numbers
and raw power, while Shogun Kaltoven has a better
intelligence network, rules over a substantially
wealthier realm, and of course… wields Vazan’s Terror.
The latter advantage might just be why this war hasn’t
exploded yet.

Tyranny on the Rise

During the dynasts’ years of decadence, the subjects of
Xiyun and Fulgurai were more or less free to conduct
their business as they wished, so long as tolls and taxes
were paid on time. In Xiyun’s land, this resulted in the
emergence of two focal points of power. The first were
the blackpowder daimyos, which were typically
common-born warlords whose influence rested in
advanced artillery, plentiful iron mines and arable land.
Second were the aristocratic templars, mortals living in
mountains far above the clouds, basing their power in
philosophy, magic and monastic prowess. The badlands
under Fulgurai’s control were much simpler, dominated
by barbarian chiefs of great martial capability, always
locking horns against one another for matters of
resources, territory, and honor.

With the fusion of their dynasts and the emergence of
the Wyrm of Blasphemy, the ecosystem of power and
influence under which these nomadic chiefs,
aristocratic monks, and industrialist warlords existed
was shaken to the core, replaced with a renewed
draconic imperium. Despite Satokai’s cultural

reverence, it would be foolish to ignore that these ruling
classes must be quite unhappy with this new
arrangement. One could even be forgiven for assuming
that seditious projects are being discussed among these
illustrious lords who had grown used to ruling without
the oversight of an emperor. In this context, all eyes are
on Kaltoven, the man who could end thousands of years
of draconic rule…

Theseus’ Notes on Legendary Foes
This Wyrm presents a real problem, my dear Eternity.
Despite its unnatural genesis, it is not some mad chimera
laying waste to everything in its path, or some reclusive
monster holding a great power hostage from those who
would use it more productively. It is a Tyrant, capital T, one
with enough brains and power to actually unify the longatomized empire of Satokai. The last time such a thing
happened, the first blackpowder weapons were invented. As
you know, ‘O void, a dozen dullards armed with such
weapons can pose a threat even to your blessed scions.

Now, it’s true that the Wyrm of Blasphemy has more
immediate interests–mainly Kaltoven–but it knows you.
Even seven thousand years later, Fulgurai and Xiyun will
hardly have forgotten their father’s killer. So enamored with
his designs they were that, despite the explicit will of their
divinity, they maintained the division of Supremacy for
thousands of years, despite having ample opportunity to
change that. And there we arrive at the crux of the problem,
dear Eternity. Late though the realization is, the Wyrm of
Blasphemy has awakened to the evident truth: Supremacy
demands unity, and its volition cannot be ignored except at
a terrible cost. Now, this tyrant labors tirelessly for total
dominion and becomes more attuned with Supremacy each
passing day. If Kaltoven falls before the Wyrm or it manages
to hunt down Anguish, we might bear witness to the rise of
a new Emperor of Eos, the sort that hasn’t existed since the
times of Agnar. This must not come to pass, lest the
material plane become a true bastion of resistance against
oblivion.

Original PDF page 194

The Wyrm of Blasphemy

Gargantuan dragon, neutral evil

Armor Class 25 (natural armor)

Hit Points 1575 (90d20 + 630)

Speed 60 ft., fly 240 ft. (hover)

STR
DEX
CON
INT
WIS
CHA

28 (+9)
28 (+9)
24 (+7)
18 (+4)
22 (+6)
12 (+1)

Saving Throws Dex +19, Con +17, Int +14, Wis +16
Skills Acrobatics +19, Arcana +14, History +14, Insight +16,
Perception +16
Damage Resistances acid; bludgeoning, piercing and
slashing from nonmagical attacks, cold, fire, thunder
Damage Immunities lightning, necrotic, poison
Condition Immunities poisoned
Senses truesight 400 ft., passive Perception 26
Recommended Level 22nd - 23rd

Proficiency Bonus +10

Limited Magic Immunity. The Wyrm cannot be affected or
detected by spells cast at 3rd level or lower unless it wishes
to be. It has advantage on saving throws against other spells
and magical effects.

Two-headed. The Wyrm has one golden head and one green
head. The Wyrm has advantage on Intelligence
(Investigation) and Wisdom (Perception) checks, as well as
on saving throws against being blinded, charmed, deafened,
frightened, stunned, and knocked unconscious.

Wielder of Supremacy. The Wyrm wields half of the Divine
Domain of Supremacy, gaining the following benefits:

Imperium: The Wyrm of Blasphemy is immune to spells and
magical effects of 8th level or lower that would compel its
behavior, such as the Command and Dominate Monster
spells. When it is targeted by such an effect that only targets
it, the Wyrm of Blasphemy can use its reaction to reflect the
effects of the spell, turning the caster into the target. All DCs
and effects remain unchanged, but if the effect requires
concentration, the Wyrm must use its own to maintain it.

Vanquisher. The Wyrm has a +10 bonus to ability checks it
rolls as part of a contest. In addition, damage dealt by the
Wyrm ignores resistance to lightning and necrotic damage
and treats immunity as if it were resistance.

Majesty. Creatures that start their turn within 120 feet of the
Wyrm of Blasphemy and can see it must succeed on a DC 25
Wisdom saving throw or be forced to kneel, dropping prone
and having their movement speed reduced to 0 until the
start of their next turn. A creature that succeeds on this
saving throw becomes immune to the Majesty trait for the
next 24 hours.

In the event of the Wyrm’s death, Supremacy will break into
1d4 + 1 shards, which will remain floating atop its corpse for
one hour before phasing into the corrupted Narak
dimension, where they will separate and travel aimlessly
across the cosmos. A Shard of Supremacy will only attune to
a creature that embodies its values with exceptional depth. A
creature that attempts to forcefully claim a Shard must make
a DC 25 Charisma saving throw, gaining control of the Shard
on a success, or taking 105 (6d6 × 5) force damage on a
failure. The DC decreases by 5 if the creature already wields
at least one shard of Supremacy, and by a further 5 if the
creature wields more than one shard. A creature that fails to
claim Divinity cannot attempt to do so again for the next 24
hours.

Legendary Foe

The Wyrm of Blasphemy has 4 Legendary Points (LP). It can
use these points to fuel certain abilities. Once it uses a
feature or action that consumes a Legendary Point, it cannot
use that same ability again until the start of its next turn. The
Wyrm of Blasphemy regains spent Legendary Points at the
start of its turn.

Legendary Resistance. When the Wyrm fails a saving throw, it
can expend 1 Legendary Point to succeed instead.

Legendary Endurance. When the Wyrm takes damage, it can
expend 1 Legendary Point to reduce the damage taken by
100.

Cleanse Effect. If the Wyrm starts its turn affected by a
condition or magical effect of 7th level or lower, it can
expend 1 Legendary Point to end the effect. If the effect or
condition is of 8th or 9th level, or from a Mythical Technique,
the Wyrm must expend 2 Legendary Points to end that effect.
Effects originating from a 10th-level spell cannot be cleansed
in this way.

Original PDF page 195

Phase Transition

If the Wyrm of Blasphemy is lowered to 500 hit points or
fewer, one of its heads withers and dies to sustain the life of
the other. The Wyrm loses its Two Headed trait and gains the
Enraged condition, gaining access to Enraged actions and
causing certain abilities to gain additional effects. When this
phase transition happens, the Wyrm is cleansed of all
harmful conditions and regains 2 Legendary Points. The
nature and targeting of the attacks suffered by the Wyrm
determine which head is destroyed, at the DM’s discretion.
Depending on the surviving head, the Wyrm gains new
abilities and features and loses others:

Head of Xiyun. If the green head survives, the Wyrm gains the
following traits:

The Wyrm’s Breath of Curses costs 1 LP instead of 2, and
its associated save DCs become 27.
+

Necrotic damage dealt by the Wyrm is doubled.
+

The Wyrm regains 50 hit points at the start of each of its
turns.
+

+

The Wyrm can no longer use the following actions:
Fulgurai’s Inferno, Fulgurating Bite, Invoke Lightning,
Multiattack, Primordial Lightning Breath, Ruin of the
Dynasts.

Head of Fulgurai. If the golden head survives, the Wyrm gains
the following traits:

The Wyrm’s Primordial Lightning Breath costs 1 LP
instead of 2, and its associated save DC becomes 30.
+

The Wyrm’s maximum fly speed becomes 480 feet.
+

+

The Wyrm can no longer use the following actions:
Breath of Curses, Necrotic Bite, Invoke Curse,
Multiattack, Ruin of the Dynasts, Xiyun’s Torment.

Actions

Multiattack. The Wyrm makes three attacks: one with its
Claws, one with its Fulgurating Bite, and one with its
Necrotic Bite.

Multiattack (Enraged). The Wyrm makes four attacks: two
with its Claws and two with its Fulgurating Bite or Necrotic
Bite, depending on which of its heads remains alive. If
available, it can replace one Bite attack with its Primordial
Lightning Breath or Breath of Curses.

Claws. Melee Weapon Attack: +19 to hit, reach 5 ft., one
target. Hit: 23 (4d6 + 9) slashing damage plus 26 (4d12)
Lethal damage.

Fulgurating Bite. Melee Weapon Attack: +19 to hit, reach 5 ft.,
one target. Hit: 23 (4d6 + 9) piercing damage plus 26 (4d12)
lightning damage. If the target is a creature, it must succeed
on a DC 27 Constitution saving throw or be stunned by the
overwhelming energy until the end of its next turn.

Necrotic Bite. Melee Weapon Attack: +19 to hit, reach 5 ft.,
one target. Hit: 23 (4d6 + 9) piercing damage plus 26 (4d12)
necrotic damage. If the target is a creature that isn’t a
construct, the Wyrm heals an amount of hit points equal to
the necrotic damage dealt.

Primordial Lightning Breath (Costs 2 LP). The Wyrm breathes
primordial lightning in a 600-foot line that is 15 feet wide.
Creatures within that area must make a DC 27 Dexterity
saving throw, taking 175 (10d6 x 5) lightning damage on a
failed save, or half as much on a successful one.

Breath of Curses (Costs 2 LP). The Wyrm uses one of the
following breath weapon options.

Paralyzing Breath. The Wyrm breathes a cloud of paralyzing
poison in a 60-foot cone in front of it. Creatures within that
area must make a DC 25 Constitution saving throw, taking
70 (4d6 x 5) poison damage and being poisoned for one
minute on a failed save, or taking half as much damage and
not being poisoned on a successful one. While poisoned in
this way, a creature is paralyzed. An affected creature can
repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns,
ending the effect on itself on a success.

Necrotic Breath. The Wyrm breathes a necrotic mist in a 60foot cone in front of it. Creatures within that area must
succeed a DC 25 Constitution saving throw, taking 35 (10d6)
necrotic damage and being cursed for one minute on a failed
save, or taking half as much damage and not being cursed
on a successful one. A cursed creature takes 35 (10d6)
necrotic damage at the start of each of its turns and
becomes vulnerable to necrotic, piercing, and slashing
damage. A Remove Curse or similar effect lifts this curse
early.

Petrifying Breath. The Wyrm breathes a cloud of petrifying
gas in a 60-foot cone in front of it. Creatures in the area must
make a DC 25 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a
creature is restrained as its flesh begins to harden. On a
successful save, the creature isn’t affected. A creature
restrained by this magic must make another Constitution
saving throw at the end of each of its turns. If it successfully
saves three times, the effect ends for it. If it fails its saves
three times, it is turned to stone and subjected to the
petrified condition. The successes and failures don’t need to
be consecutive; keep track of both until the target collects
three of a kind. If the creature is physically broken while
petrified, it suffers from similar deformities if it reverts to its
original state.

Original PDF page 196

Bonus Actions

Invoke Lightning. The Wyrm causes a pillar of lightning to fall
in a 60-foot-tall, 15-foot radius cylinder centered on a point it
can see within 120 feet. Creatures in the area must make a
DC 27 Dexterity saving throw, taking 35 (10d6) lightning
damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful
one. If the Wyrm is Enraged, it can choose to teleport to the
point it chooses, pushing objects and creatures to the
nearest unoccupied space after they suffer the lightning
damage.

Invoke Curse. The Wyrm invokes a ring of cursed energy in a
30-foot diameter circle centered on a point it can see within
120 feet. Creatures in that area must make a DC 25 Wisdom
saving throw or be cursed for 1 minute. While cursed in this
way, a creature has disadvantage on attacks against the
Wyrm and on saving throws caused by the Wyrm. If the
Wyrm is Enraged, creatures afflicted by this curse can use
one action or one bonus action on their turn, but not both. A
Remove Curse or similar effect lifts this curse early.

Extra Actions

The Wyrm of Blasphemy can take extra actions outside of its
turn by expending Legendary Points, choosing from the options
below. Only one extra action can be used at a time and only at
the end of another creature’s turn.

Onslaught (Costs 1 LP). The Wyrm makes one attack with its
Claws.

Fly (Costs 1 LP). The Wyrm moves up to its maximum speed
in a direction of its choosing.

DM Notes

Combining the story and abilities of two separate beings
into a single stat sheet, the Wyrm of Blasphemy is
perhaps the most complex monster of the Paragon tier.
Running this creature at the table need not be an ordeal,
however, if you give these pages a thorough read and
keep a few things in mind while playing it.

An Overbearing Foe

Spurred by its Divine Domain and the traumatic fall of the
Ruby Dynasty, the Wyrm of Blasphemy is a creature that
strives for immediate control over any given situation,
including combat. This pulsion is a strength and a
weakness: it will help it prevail in scenarios where
decisive action is an advantage, while severely
disadvantaging it in scenarios where caution is the
winning strategy. What this means mechanically is that
the Wyrm of Blasphemy is prone to overspend LPs early
in the combat round (likely using Ruin of the Dynasts) and
leaving itself open to retaliation if the party remains whole
enough to punch back, which it probably will. The Wyrm

Reflective Scales (Costs 2 LP). The Wyrm enchants its scales
with the powers of abjuration and reflection, gaining a +3
bonus to its AC until the end of its next turn. For the
duration, when the Wyrm is targeted by a magic missile
spell, a line spell, or a spell that requires a ranged attack roll,
roll a d6. On a 1 to 5, the Wyrm is unaffected. On a 6, the
Wyrm is unaffected, and the effect is reflected back at the
caster as though it originated from the target of this spell,
turning the caster into the target. If the Wyrm is Enraged,
spells are reflected on a roll of 3-6.

Fulgurai’s Inferno (Enraged, Costs 3 LP). The Wyrm uses its
Primordial Lightning Breath. After that, 5 bolts of lightning fall
from the skies in 60-foot-tall, 15-foot radius cylinders
centered on points it can see within 120 feet. The points
cannot be placed in a way that would make the resulting
cylinders overlap. Creatures within those areas must make a
DC 27 Dexterity saving throw, taking 35 (10d6) lightning
damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful
one.

Xiyun’s Torment (Enraged, Costs 3 LP). The Wyrm brutally
rends the scales and muscles of its chest, revealing the
putrid draconid heart beneath, a misshapen alchemical
fusion that thrums with a mind-rending beat. It takes 100
Lethal damage that cannot be reduced or prevented in any
way. At the end of each of the Wyrm’s turns, creatures within
480 feet of the Wyrm and not behind full cover must make a
DC 25 Wisdom saving throw, taking 35 (10d6) psychic
damage and 70 (20d6) Dark damage on a failed save, or half
as much on a successful one. This effect lasts until the Wyrm
dies or heals back to its maximum hit points.

Ruin of the Dynasts (Costs 4 LP). The Wyrm uses its Primordial
Lightning Breath and its Breath of Curses at the same time, in
whichever order it chooses.

will try to dictate a frenetic tempo for the battle,
rapidly spending itself while inflicting grievous
wounds on its enemies.
Surviving Shock & Awe

To prevail against an overbearing foe like the
Wyrm of Blasphemy, the party will probably have
to prepare defensive measures beyond the basic
toolkit of all legendary heroes–namely, Legendary
Resistances. Powerful damage mitigation is highly
rewarded, as is avoiding AoE-exploitable positioning.
This last part should be evident to a party facing a
draconic enemy, but as far as its other weak points go,
they should not be too hard to glean, either. The Wyrm of
Blasphemy is a high-profile continental ruler, after all, not
a reclusive necromancer of whom little is known. And… it
has made plenty of enemies in its short time as an
absolute monarch, most of them members of its own
court. Furtive encounters under the cover of night can
serve as a prelude to the confrontation, where NPCs that
have a bone to pick with the blasphemous dynast can
reveal weaknesses and viable strategies to the party.

pages.name: p. 197 - Of potential allies, the wisest and strongest would be

text.content:
Original PDF page 197

Of potential allies, the wisest and strongest would be
Kaltoven, if you are playing within the Universe of
Strings, or whoever you designate as the wielder of
Vazan’s Terror in your own game. We recommend
playing this character with ambivalence towards the
dynasts, wishing the party well but emotionally or
pragmatically unwilling to use every last measure to aid
them. Strategic counsel should be the bulk of this
character’s help, unless they are convinced to assume a
more active position.

Dual Personality

Even though both are taken by Supremacy, the two
minds of the dynasts still clash within the Wyrm of
Blasphemy. Fulgurai is by far the most rapturous, while
Xiyun has historically been academically inclined and
cautious. This duality provides more than just flavor:
depending on the course the fight is taking, it can be
used to modulate the strategies adopted by the Wyrm in
a narratively satisfying way. If the Wyrm’s recklessness
leads it to take a massive amount of damage, for
example, Xiyun’s head can take prominence, causing it
to be more conservative. Similarly, if the fight is going
too well for the Wyrm of Blasphemy, this tool of
regulation can be employed to make it commit mistakes
or leave itself open to equalizing blows.

For this reason–and to make the Phase Transition all
the more satisfying–we recommend establishing the
differentiation between the two heads early on, to avoid
springing such revelations on your players in the midst
of battle. It can be teased in prelude encounters, and
introduced at the beginning of the fight–with the two
heads disagreeing, for example.

Tiers of Play

The Wyrm of Blasphemy is a very challenging
Legendary Foe for parties at the Paragon tier, so it can
be a relatively engaging challenge for parties beyond
that level as well. In its intended tier, this Legendary
Foe can generate casualties very easily. You might want
to pull your punches if that’s not the kind of battle you
are looking for.

Legend

With its aggressive demeanor and overbearing AoE
capabilities, the Wyrm of Blasphemy will completely
overwhelm even the most clever parties at this tier,
unless they are outfitted with purpose-made magic
items such as Vazan’s Terror. As mentioned in other
Paragon-tier foes, the jump between 21st level and
22nd level is incredibly meaningful for Legendary
Characters, making a Paragon-tier foe (and especially
the Wyrm of Blasphemy) not entirely suited for this kind
of encounter.

Paragon

The Wyrm stands as one of the strongest foes in its
intended tier of play. This level of might doesn’t take it
to a degree where it becomes an unfair fight for
characters, but the potential for catastrophic defeat is
higher. So if failure is not a true option at the table you
run, we urge caution. Keep an eye on your players’ hit
points and available Legendary Points, and play

A Wandering Hermit

Tales tell of a hooded traveler, a one-eyed hermit, as old
as he is wise, who wanders through paths best left
forgotten and helps adventurers in their quests,
wherever he might find them. He is a shadow of eld,
present in the folklore of every culture of Eos and
beyond, always arriving when he is needed most, before
disappearing in a puff of radiant dust, never to be seen
again.

Of Divinity

Divine domains are something of a confusing topic in a
universe of dead gods. Clerics and paladins all across
the planes of existence manifest their magic through
shades, aspects, even remembrances of the ancient
pantheon; for even in death, these divine domains have
endured, first and foremost in the form of Threads of
Divinity, aspects of reality made manifest. They coexist,
in one way or another, with their worshipers–that is the
basis of divine magic; to be a catalyst for the intrinsic
power held by concepts.

The Gaard of the Nova Cluster count many clerics
among their ranks, all devoted to Order, even though
Gyreon, the god they worship, is naught but stardust
and lingering energy. Odin is a corrupt shadow of his
former self, yet some paladins call upon the golden
magic of the Thread of War, as if the Allfather still led
the Einherjar legions through the cosmos. The dwarven
priests of Dur’Vatar commune, and sometimes even
visit, the Halls of Kallemtur, the dwarven afterlife, even
though the God of the Forge has been dead for
thousands of years.

The god-like creature known as the Hermit is not one of
these cases. This strange character appears in many
cultures all across the universe, in different aspects.
Some consider it a stranger, an embodiment of death.
Some, instead, prefer to think of it as a traveler, a wise
man, passing through and providing help. The
enigmatic entity is believed to be a myth, for no one can
truly corroborate its existence, and yet, somehow, those
who devote themselves to it are blessed with divine
power that rivals and often surpasses that of the
previous examples.
The Hooded Man

Depending on where and, more importantly, from
whom you learn the Hermit, he might embody many
different mythological figures. To the northern realms
that worship broken Asgard, he is one-eyed Odin,
surveilling his flock, noting who might be worthy of the
Einherjar title beyond death. This is a very common
interpretation, even beyond the north, for it explains the
Hermit’s penchant for good and the aid he extends to
the many heroes he comes across in myth.

More experienced planeswalkers, particularly those
who have visited broken Asgard and seen Odin’s
predicament, know this interpretation to be false. Yet, in
the Hermit’s every appearance around the Northern

Realms, he is always described as a hooded man with a
long white beard, and damningly, only one good eye,
which fits precisely with the common interpretations of
Odin.
The Golden Staff

The Hermit’s lore gets increasingly more complicated
when considering that, in Kahar, the great continent of
southern Eos, he takes a different form entirely. No
longer is he a one-eyed old man, but rather a hardened
and handsome traveler, with a golden cord around his
forehead, and a solemn, unshakable expression of grit.
He walks beneath the scorching sun of the deserts as if
Nemeq, Lion God of Kahar, blessed his path–indeed, he
is mistaken for Nemeq himself by many wanderers.
Much like the northern interpretation, this mysterious
man appears in many myths all across the southern
continent of the material plane, from the Golden Coast
to the Rhazaali Jungles, and fulfills the same role as the
North’s hooded man, assisting heroes and guiding them
through hardship.

There are more such interpretations. The
Iskendarians see the Hermit as a mysterious noble
benefactor, dressed in fine clothing and hidden behind a
golden sun mask. The minotaur tribes of the Soulfrost
Expanse tell of a bull-headed wanderer, hidden beneath
heavy furs and almost impervious to even the harshest
snowstorms, as if he were their God Tauryl himself. The
people of the dwarf planet Aeryn, far beyond the
material plane, depict the Hermit as an angel draped in
white and blue, able to walk through the characteristic
starfalls of their tumultuous planet and helping those
caught in the plains during such celestial events.

These many figures are all connected by one single
element: a two-pronged, golden staff. Every myth of the
Hermit has this mysterious man use his golden staff to
part the many obstacles he comes across, from starfalls
to snowstorms, to even oceans, helping the heroes of
said tales reach their destination, wherever that might
be.
Myriel Genra’s Pursuit

Threadseeker of Divinity Myriel Genra, accomplished
envoy of the City of Strings, has taken upon themselves
the task of discovering the Hermit’s mysterious origin,
and, perhaps, among the stars, find the entity
responsible for so many events all across the universe.
In this pursuit, Genra has compiled all the information
that could pertain to the Hermit in one academic work,
and after many years of investigation, sent their report
on such a being to the City of Strings. It seems that, in
their search, Myriel Genra managed to pierce the veil
that hid the creature, being granted an audience with
the divine being’s true form.

Original PDF page 201

Of Many Interpretations

From the writings of Myriel Genra, the Council of
Threadmasters has concluded that the Hermit is, in
fact, an extremely powerful, benevolent entity that
wanders across the cosmos, manifesting around
mortals of great potential and, through a disguise meant
to elicit cooperation, aiding them in fulfilling said
potential. Its similarities with many mythological
characters in each culture it visits are no coincidence; in
fact, it seems the Hermit purposefully takes on traits of
heroes that would be recognized to reassure those he
aids.

It is interesting to note that, in its true, astral-faring
form described by Myriel Genra, the Hermit has a
golden mask for a face. Deceit and false appearances
seem to be ingrained in the creature’s very nature. Its
body is that of a long, dragon-like monster, which, at
first, pushed the theory that this creature was Leung,
the Wyrm of Supremacy. This was quickly discredited
by the simple fact that Leung’s domain is still being
wielded by the Divine Dragons of Satokai, which makes
it impossible for the Hermit to lend his powers to clerics
from across the cosmos, especially when considering
the whims of Supremacy itself. No, the Hermit must be
something greater even than that.
A Potential Answer

Another theory, pushed by Threadmaster Fallow during
the Council’s discussion of Genra’s findings, has the
entity as the shadow of Ylwe, greatest of the Xildur, God
of Stars, and King of the Pantheon; a remnant that
somehow survived the deity’s demise during the Age of
the Gods. The similarities are quite striking; Ylwe is
said to have taken a draconic form to mate with the
Mother of Chaos, Tiamat, creating the mythical Yrith
Dragons, some of which are still alive to this day. This
would explain the Hermit’s draconic form and his
immense power over light and stars. In addition, Ylwe
was a great teacher of heroes, and guided them through
hardship, testing their mettle for some
incomprehensible purpose. The Hermit is depicted
doing exactly that in his every form, helping the many
heroes of the cosmos to greater heights.

But how could the God of Gods have survived? Ylwe
was famously murdered, betrayed in a mythological
event that would lead to the War of Eternity and the Fall
of the Gods. If the Hermit truly was Ylwe, wouldn’t he
have stopped such a catastrophic chain of events? The
entity is definitely enigmatic, but it seems to fight
against the Void and its minions. If Ylwe had survived,
he would have done all in his power to avoid the events
that occurred after–and indeed, because of–his demise.
Yet, the Hermit was nowhere to be found during the Age
of Gods. Its earliest depiction–at least from Myriel
Genra’s studies–is in the Teng myth of Atizai the Green
and the First Lich Belm, occurring several centuries
into the Second Age, far from the War of Eternity.

From “To See a God”, Chapter
Nine

Through the hundreds of frequencies that overlap our
universe, I chased the faintest echoes of my mark. I walked
through the paths of the Hermit itself, in the dark recesses
of our Universe and even beyond, where no light escapes
the shadow of the Enemy.

When I realized we both derived our power from the
same ghost, I understood how to chase such an elusive
figure. In its worship of the Brother, I could finally get a hold
of its after-image. And thus, I pursued it. Through smoke
and mirrors, golden effigies of forgotten civilizations, faded
memories of the Universe that was, and a thousand
different destinies all occurring at once, I found it. Among a
sea of stars, the dragon-like creature finally acknowledged
my efforts by turning to face me. It could run no longer–or
rather, it understood I would never relent. I had discovered
the currents it used to travel unnoticed across the cosmos,
and could now follow it even to its starry lair.

And so, it spoke to me. An echo, a ghost. It was brief, yet
overpowering in its speech.

THOU DESIREST TO BE PROVEN.

THOU ART PERSISTENT.
BY THE BROTHER, SO BE IT.
THY TRIAL HATH BEGUN, REGAN RIMLEY.

Despite my attempts to appease it, the Hermit prepared to
fight. Our battle was short–I could not withstand its might–
yet it did not destroy me. I awoke in a particular location in
Pandemonium called the Garden of Eden, under the
watchful eye of Zedekiah, the Teacher of Heroes. Everything,
it seems, had been a test. Did I… pass? Or did I fail?

– Myriel Genra, Threadseeker of Divinity

The Hermit’s Lair

Unlike other Legendary Foes in this book, the Hermit
cannot be fought outside of his lair. He will only do
battle against those who find him, through the folds of
space and time.

The Scar of Divinity

The Hermit’s lair has only been visited by Myriel Genra
in known history. In their writings following their
encounter with the creature, the Threadseeker of
Divinity has named that cradle of light the Scar of
Divinity. A cosmic expanse of a billion stars, where
celestial bodies are born and die, comets streak across
the heavenly canvas, inhabited only by the Hermit. A
Universe within the Universe.

It is not a physical location–no amount of space-faring
would allow someone to reach the Scar of Divinity. It is
more akin to a pocket dimension, a frequency beyond
even the ethereal plane, where all planes converge,
folded in a single space. Like an onion, the Scar of
Divinity seems to be layered in such a way that the
Hermit never needs to leave it; indeed, it may even be
incapable of doing so. It always delves deeper and
deeper, like a serpent burrowing in cosmic sand.

Within the Scar of Divinity, all planes of existence can
be accessed with a simple thought. There are even gates
to places beyond the known Universe, which leads
many among the City of Strings to believe the Scar of
Divinity might stretch through the void, and into
unknowable dimensions. The Hermit could very well be
of another reality.
Genra’s Theories

The Scar isn’t truly explainable with the current
understanding of the cosmos that the City of Strings
teaches. All theories are merely conjecture.

This said, Myriel Genra has established that the Scar
of Divinity cannot be older than the Fall of the Gods,
and must date to a point right after the War of Eternity.
It is clear that the Hermit did not exist before the Fall,
and neither did the Scar. The first of his many legends,
featuring Atizai the Green, dates to several hundreds of
years after the War of Eternity, which could well and
truly be the origin of the Hermit’s well-documented
assisting of heroes.

The Scar is named after the fall of the Gods, for it
seems that, somehow, the Hermit was born out of that
grand tragedy. This dimension, connecting all planes, is
an opening, a defect of reality itself, probably created by
the death of elder deities and the loss of aspects of
reality we cannot even conceive of, in this wounded
Universe. Genra managed to access it only by following
the remnants of the Threads of Divinity; they all seem to
connect back to this cradle of cosmic light. Perhaps only
Wielders of Divinity are capable of following the threads
in such a way as to find the Scar?
The Garden of Eden

The Scar is intrinsically connected to every plane in the
universe, yet of all possible locations to pick, those that
are proven unworthy of the Test are banished to the
Garden of Eden, where another known teacher of
Heroes resides. The relation between the Hermit and
Zedekiah is unclear, though they must be cooperating in
some way against the efforts of the Void. The Hermit’s
work within the Scar of Divinity must be of vital
importance to the survival of the Universe, for it is
protected by the very ghost of Tiamat. Both beings seem
to be specters of Gods, after all.

Theseus’ Notes on Legendary Foes
We do not require the great wars of elder times to vanquish.
The universe is a darker, colder place than before, yet it has
not drawn its final breath. Finesse incites much less unified
resistance than brute force, my dear Eternity. Believe me, I
know a thing or two about slaying noble monsters.

The Hermit will be a difficult challenge to face, yet one
that must be met if we are to succeed. He will attempt to
flee within his pocket dimension, and time and time again,
we must endure his overpowering resolve to push us away.
We must pursue relentlessly until the Hermit has nowhere
else to hide. In this vein, we must find a way to withstand
the banishing energies of that bleeding dimension. An
artifact that would shield us from planar travel would be
paramount. If not, there is no way to follow the Hermit
through his infinite planes, and we will find ourselves in
front of Zedekiah to face judgment. Do not worry, dear
Eternity. We will slay her, too.

Perhaps another important factor in the defeat of the
Hermit is the tool used for the job. Our arsenal is full of
such artifacts; I recommend the Black Fate Armaments that
the Teng so kindly forged for us. Without such means, the
Hermit will disappear within the stars, never to be seen
again.

We only have one shot, dear Eternity. If the Hermit feels
threatened, he will close all gates to his lair for an Age. We
must slay the beast. I can take this task upon myself. It
would not be the first time I delved into a labyrinth.
