Accessing the Scar

Only Wielders of Divinity can divine a path to the Scar.
An Astral Projection ritual must be channeled by one or
more Wielders of Divinity, in which several trials of the
mind, created specifically to test each of the would-be
contestants’ resolve, must be completed. These
challenges revisit parts of the Wielders’ lives, which
allows the Hermit to make a judgment about each
contestant’s character. Once they are deemed worthy of
an audience, the Astral Projection spell is completed,
and every individual who took part in the ritual is
transported to the Scar, regardless of whether they
possess a Thread of Divinity or not.

There might be other ways to access the Scar without
requiring the approval of the Hermit, yet the astral
creature does not take well to intrusions. A Divine Rift
spell, for example, can open the way without the need
for an Astral Projection, making the Hermit’s Test more
easily accessible, and maintain a path through the
planes unto the Scar even if the Hermit banishes or
slays the contestants.

Regional Effects

The Scar of Divinity is a virtually infinite space akin to
the astral sea, filled with half-born stars, shadows of the
void, and rifts in reality that connect to a thousand
different planes of existence. Distance is relative in such
a wide arena, and the Hermit is in control of the space
between all who are currently in his domain. The
following effects characterize this region.

Astral Space. Creatures within the Scar of Divinity do
not require air, food, drink, or sleep. There is no gravity
within the Hermit’s Lair, and all creatures have a flying
speed equal to their Intelligence modifier times 10
(minimum of 10 feet), unless their fly speed is higher
without this bonus.

Time Dilation. Time passes differently within the Scar
of Divinity. A day outside of the Scar is equal to two
days within the Hermit’s lair.

Hermit’s Domain. Provided the Hermit has enough
time, he can create anything within his domain, from
stars to environments to even new realities, as if casting
the Wish spell multiple times. These creations take the
Hermit 1 minute per cubic meter of space he chooses to
alter within his domain. If he channels this ability for an
hour or more, he begins to alter space at a rate of 10
cubic meters per minute. If he channels this ability for
24 hours or more, this rate becomes 100 cubic meters
per minute. Everything the Hermit creates turns to
stardust if the Hermit stops actively paying attention to
it, which makes the Hermit’s Lair an enormous canvas
of unfinished creation. The Hermit will rarely create
something that does not pertain to his present situation
and the tests he is currently conducting.

failure, they take 45 (10d8) force damage that cannot be
mitigated in any way, or half as much damage on a
success. This effect reduces a creature’s maximum hit
points by an amount equal to the damage taken.

Misanthropy. The Hermit tires of the battle; the
actions of the heroes are not interesting enough for him.
He expels all creatures from his domain and burrows
deeper within the Scar of Divinity. All creatures
currently within the Scar of Divinity must succeed on a
DC 25 Charisma saving throw or be banished from the
pocket dimension. The Hermit can choose to teleport
them to the Garden of Eden, in the Broken Hells.

As part of this action, the Hermit casts plane shift on
himself and teleports to a deeper layer of his domain,
creating a rift in his last location. Other creatures can
touch this rift to be teleported to the same layer the
Hermit is currently in. For every round that passes after
the first, the rift begins to close, gaining a cumulative
33% chance of failing, which would result in the target
being banished to a random location in the Astral Sea.
After three rounds have passed, the rift closes for good.

When the Hermit uses this action, he creates an
additional layer of his domain, leaving the one with the
characters and tests behind. The new layer is exactly
the same as the last one with only small alterations. The
layer that was left behind begins to crumble, no longer
supported by the Hermit’s attention: when the Hermit’s
rift closes in the crumbling layer, it is destroyed and all
creatures within that layer are banished to a random
location in the Astral Sea.

A character can use the Plane Shift spell to hop
between layers without the need to reach the Hermit’s
Rift, nor the danger of failure.

DM Notes

The Hermit is one of the most powerful creatures in this
book (and in the Universe of Strings, for that matter),
and is supposed to face characters of 25th level, with
tremendous power-ups, and indeed, some sort of divine
boon. Pitting such a foe against your players in a godslaying adventure can be as fun as it can be difficult. But
just because the Hermit is extremely strong doesn’t
mean you can’t make use of him as part of a tamer
encounter! The following are some notes on how to run
this monster, along with several possible resolutions.

Victory Conditions

The Hermit is, by all intents and purposes, a good guy.
He isn’t going to pose a deadly threat to any character,
unless your party’s goal is to kill him. The encounter is
supposed to last until the Hermit reaches 66% of his
health, or around 1300 hit points. This marks the end of
the battle for most parties. If you think your group can
manage a bigger challenge, forgo the first phase
transition and fight until the Hermit reaches 33% of his
health, or around 700 hit points.

Passing the Hermit’s “test” should be accompanied
by some sort of reward; in the case of our own Universe,
the party is blessed by the Hermit and rewarded with
great secrets about legendary techniques and certain
10th-level spells. The Hermit can be a really good
encounter to prepare for a potential final fight against a
truly evil BBEG, at the end of a campaign.
Killing the Hermit

If, for some reason, your party must destroy the Hermit,
reducing him to 0 hit points is quite the endeavor. He
must be hunted through several dimensions, casting
Plane Shift right as he escapes deeper into the Scar of
Divinity. The would-be assassins must also manage to
resist the many banishment spells the Hermit will throw
at them.

Once the Hermit reaches 0 hit points, however, he
will not simply die–he must be destroyed by a weapon of
the void. In our universe, some of the Relics presented
in this book can accomplish such a task.

The Challenge

If you want to run a true 25th-level challenge, there
must be much more to do than simply attacking the
boss. Most of the time, defeating the enemy is the
byproduct of a more important goal being
accomplished.

For these scenarios, the Hermit’s trial should not be
your conventional battle encounter. Rather than fighting
to the death, the Hermit might attempt to escape the
party, burrowing deeper into its domain and pushing the
heroes out with a variety of spells and attacks.

The party fails the trial not by falling to 0 hit points,
but by failing to complete the test before the Hermit
becomes unreachable.

Tests of Might and Cunning. As the party arrives at
the Hermit’s lair, they will be subject to several tests—
puzzles constructed by the Hermit—they must pass
while battling the Legendary Foe. Should they succeed
at least three of the tests, they can either gain the
Hermit’s favor, or, if you are feeling especially
challenging, go unto the Trial of Shadows.

The party can also choose to prove its worthiness
through battle; every time the Hermit uses its Phase
Transition as a result of taking damage, the party counts
as having completed one test.

The Trial of Shadows. After completing three tests,
the party may reach the final phase; they are teleported
to a smaller arena in which the Hermit’s Projection
summons a copy of each character through the Greater
Simulacrum 10th-level spell. The party must overcome
their evil dopplegangers and defeat the Hermit one final
time to win the god’s favor.
The Tests

When preparing the Hermit’s encounter, try to design
Tests suited to your party’s strengths and story, creating
encounters or puzzles that reward their specific abilities
and recollection of lore. Each Test can be located in
different parts of the arena, accessed through planar
doors that lead to isolated demiplanes.

While within a Test’s demiplane, the characters
should not be able to interact with the Hermit, nor be
affected by his abilities.

Perhaps, at some point, the Hermit might be all alone
in the arena, as all characters are busy taking the tests.
If no creature remains within the Hermit’s domain to
engage him, he is likely to use his Misanthropy lair
action, which means the entire layer the party inhabits
begins to crumble. See Misanthropy for more
information about the Scar’s layers.

If the entire party leaves a layer, any ongoing Test in
that layer fails. However, a Test completed while a layer
is collapsing still counts toward the Trial’s completion.

Original PDF page 207

Tiers of Play

The Hermit’s stat block, as it is, represents an
Ascended-tier challenge, one of the highest you can
feasibly run in Dungeons and Dragons. It is the closest
you can get to fighting an actual God within the
Universe of Strings! You can, however, scale the
encounter back to almost all tiers of epic level play,
making for an engaging encounter regardless of your
party’s current strength.
Legend

Perhaps you are just beginning your foray into
Legendary Play, which means your party is at most 20th
or 21st level, with appropriate legendary boons and
incredibly powerful artifacts. For such a battle, keeping
the Hermit’s move set a little simpler, and taking out
most, if not all, of its 10th-level shenanigans, goes a long
way to make it a more manageable fight.

Its damage output is also something to consider when
fighting parties of 21st level or lower. Dial back some of
the Hermit’s attacks, such as its meteoric bonus action
Hurl Star, capable of dishing out consistent damage
across multiple turns, or its Voice of the Stars breath
weapon, dealing an insane amount of radiant damage.

Paragon

Legendary Characters of the Paragon tier can face this
creature without the need for too many alterations–just
remember to exercise restraint when pushing your
advantage. The Hermit is testing the characters; he
wants to see how well they can manage against a foe as
powerful as himself. Slaying the heroes can very much
take them out of the combat (destroying their astral
projection), while not necessarily ending in a player
character’s death. This can ramp up the tension as, one
by one, the heroes are returned to their bodies, judged
unworthy by the Hermit.

23rd-level characters start to have an actual shot at
pushing through the first of the Hermit’s Phase
Transitions–this can vary depending on your party’s
level of optimization.

Ascended

At 24th and 25th level, Legendary Characters have a
wide array of Legendary Resistances, trump cards that
allow them to cheat death, and guarantees beyond what
even this Legendary Foe can do. In our testing, a fully
prepared 25th-level party can deal with the Hermit quite
comfortably in a straight-up fight. We recommend
creating an engaging challenge as per the previous
section of these notes.

For an evil campaign, actually killing the Hermit is
truly the ultimate challenge, heralding the end of the
cosmos as we know it. The Hermit can only be slain
under specific circumstances; it is on you to decide how
that catastrophic event would take place!
